3957.summary: This project explores enhancing Large Language Models (LLMs) for solving cryptic crosswords, a complex reasoning benchmark where LLMs currently perform poorly (8.9% vs 99% human accuracy). The key tasks are benchmarking NorwAI's LLMs against state-of-the-art models and creating Norwegian cryptic crossword datasets, either synthetically or via rights holders. Expected outcomes include improved LLM methods for difficult reasoning tasks and a new Norwegian dataset. Co-supervision is provided by Lars Bungum (NTNU).

3958.title: Comparing Synthetic to Real Alignment Data
3958.summary: Research area: NLP, focusing on alignment of Norwegian Large Language Models (LLMs) to address data scarcity. Technologies: LLMs from TrustLLM/NorLLM, synthetic data generation, qualitative and quantitative evaluation methods. Expected outcomes: A comparative analysis of synthetic versus real alignment data effectiveness, operationalizing the problem into testable experiments to inform practices for low-resource language model alignment.

3959.title: Uncertainty Quantification for Language Models in Safety-Critical Systems
3959.summary: This project develops uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for language models to enhance reliability in safety-critical applications. The student will review literature, implement and compare UQ techniques (e.g., deep learning, Tsetlin machines, probabilistic models) across NLP benchmark datasets, and evaluate their effectiveness in quantifying uncertainty. The research will critically examine how UQ can mitigate hallucinations and misinformation, contributing to more trustworthy AI systems for domains like risk management, in collaboration with DNV's Digital Assurance program.

3962.title: Recognizing meta-data on car tires
3962.summary: Research area: Computer vision for automated tire analysis. Technologies: Deep learning models trained on a large dataset of tire images from multiple angles. Expected outcomes: Build models to predict tire metadata (size, manufacturer), estimate tread/profile depth, and detect damages, replacing manual inspection processes.

3965.title: Processing and analysing ice data
3965.summary: Research area: Processing and analyzing satellite-based ice data for Arctic and Baltic Sea regions within maritime shipping and resource extraction sectors. Technologies: Earth Observation satellite data, remote sensing, and data analysis tools. Expected outcomes: To derive insights on satellite data needs, current services, and usage patterns, contributing to the EO4BAS project led by DNV under ESA/EC funding and publishing findings in project reports.

3970.title: Applying LLM technologies to Parallel Computing
3970.summary: This project explores how Large Language Models (LLMs) and AI can advance High-Performance Computing (HPC) and parallel programming, reversing the typical narrative. Research areas include using LLMs as "co-pilots" for writing and optimizing parallel code and potentially for educational tools. The initial phase involves a literature survey. After this, the specific focus will be refined based on student interest, with expected outcomes being a deeper understanding of AI's role in improving parallel programming workflows and tools.

3980.title: FlexEEG decoding of hand movement intention
3980.summary: This project decodes hand movement intentions (e.g., grasping) from low-density EEG signals combined with source reconstruction. It involves recording multi-participant EEG data, building offline/online classifiers using machine/deep learning, and developing a software game interface. The expected outcome is a foundational wearable system with few electrodes for neurorehabilitation therapy and brain-computer interfaces.

3982.title: Developing an autofocus for 3D shape matching algorithms
3982.summary:
Research Area: 3D computer vision, focusing on automating scale selection for shape matching algorithms.

Technologies: 3D surface comparison, point correspondence detection, scale-invariant feature analysis.

Expected Outcomes: An algorithm that automatically determines the optimal scale parameter for matching 3D surfaces, analogous to camera autofocus. It must ensure consistent scale estimation for corresponding points despite surface alterations, improving robustness for applications like robotic place recognition.

3985.title: Partial encryption of video
3985.summary: This project focuses on partial video encryption for surveillance privacy, targeting objects like faces or license plates. It involves object recognition to identify regions, encrypting compressed 8x8 pixel blocks using XOR with one-time keys, and transcoding with modified Huffman trees to maintain standard player compatibility. Key outcomes include a designed filter, comprehensive testing for stream integrity and playback, and performance analysis regarding time and compression ratio.

3986.title: AI and Game based learning (multiple projects)
3986.summary: This project focuses on creating an AI system for game-based math learning that uses wearable sensors to detect student difficulties and provide timely support, addressing the "cold start problem." Technologies include AI, wearable sensors, and Python/Java programming. The student will implement the system, conduct usability and effectiveness user studies, and analyze results. Expected outcomes are a functional feedback tool and evaluated improvements in learning outcomes, requiring skills in system design and basic machine learning.

3987.title: Enhancing Online Laboratory Simulations Through AI-Powered Learning Analytics
3987.summary: This project develops AI-based learning analytics models to track and enhance student engagement and performance in online STEM laboratory simulations built with Articulate Storyline 360. The research will generate actionable insights from learner interaction data to inform real-time interventions and improve instructional design strategies, aiming to boost educational outcomes through data-driven personalization.

3988.title: Integrating AI Chatbots into Articulate Storyline 360 for Scaffolding Online Learning
3988.summary: Research Area: Educational technology, focusing on AI-enhanced online learning. Technologies: AI-powered chatbots integrated into Articulate Storyline 360 simulations. Expected Outcomes: Evaluate chatbot effectiveness in providing real-time scaffolding to address learner challenges (e.g., complex tasks), analyze interaction data to assess improvements in learning outcomes, and provide insights into using conversational AI for personalized online education.

3989.title: Evaluating the Role of Gamified Simulations in Enhancing Learner Motivation and Retention
3989.summary: This project investigates gamified simulations in online education, using Articulate Storyline 360 to build adaptive AI-driven learning experiences. It will evaluate how personalized gamification affects learner motivation, engagement, and knowledge retention to address challenges in digital learning environments.

3990.title: Smidig transformasjon
3990.summary: This project studies agile transformation, scaling agile methods from software development teams to entire organizations. Research area: organizational change management in systems development. Technologies: agile frameworks, decentralized decision-making, and customer collaboration. Expected outcomes: a literature review connecting agile transformation to change management theories, followed by an empirical case study of organizations implementing agile at scale, potentially leveraging findings from the Agile 2.0 competence project.

3991.title: Teamwork in software development
3991.summary: Research area: Teamwork in agile and distributed software development teams. Methods: Initial literature review of team performance models and empirical studies, potentially extended to an empirical study on factors affecting team effectiveness. Technologies: Agile methodologies and virtual collaboration tools. Expected outcomes: Synthesis of existing research and identification of key factors to improve team efficiency and effectiveness in software development.

3992.title: Customized Master Projects in Basic Machine Learning Research
3992.summary: This project offers flexible, open-ended Master's theses in foundational machine learning research. Key research areas include algorithm development (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement learning), theoretical analysis, and investigating model robustness, explainability, fairness, or optimization. Students will gain hands-on experience with ML programming and frameworks, working on specific problems like efficient self-attention, self-supervised learning for tabular data, or image generation quality assessment. Under mentorship, the expected outcome is contributing to cutting-edge research, honing technical skills, and potentially publishing findings. Suitable for those with foundational ML knowledge and a curiosity for core research challenges.

3993.title: Scaling algorithms for databases
3993.summary: This project focuses on evaluating modern hashing algorithms for data scaling in distributed databases. Building on prior theoretical work, the research will involve practical implementation and benchmarking to determine which algorithms perform best in scalable, distributed environments. The expected outcome is a set of practical recommendations for selecting optimal scaling techniques in real-world distributed database systems.

3994.title: XAI og bakdører i maskinlæringsmodeller
3994.summary: Research area: Investigate cryptographic backdoors in machine learning models using explainable AI (XAI) for detection. Technologies: Machine learning, cryptography, XAI methods, programming. Expected outcomes: Understand existing techniques, evaluate backdoor detectability, explore alternative constructions. Collaboration: Joint work with mathematics and computer science students. Suitable for motivated students with programming, math, and cryptography skills.

3995.title: Graph compression and indexing
3995.summary: This project focuses on designing compact data structures for large-scale graph management. Research involves two areas: graph compression, exploiting redundancy in datasets like genomics and web graphs using techniques such as differential encoding, and graph indexing, specifically for efficient reachability queries. The goal is to create space-efficient structures that enable fast querying on massive graphs, addressing scalability limitations of traditional methods for applications in bioinformatics, social networks, and beyond. Expected outcomes include novel succinct data structures for various graph classes and practical indexing solutions for real-world graph databases.

3997.title: VIRTUAL DEVICES ACROSS OPERATING SYSTEMS
3997.summary: This project researches developing virtual camera and microphone devices for Linux, Mac OS, and Android. Using Python and C/C++, it aims to create applications for digital meetings with effects like voice change and facial characteristic modification. Builds on visual computing and graphics visualization knowledge from courses such as TDT4195 or TDT4230.

3998.title: RECONSTRUCTION OF NIDAROSDOMEN BASED ON GAUSSIAN PRIMITIVES
3998.summary: This master's project involves reconstructing Nidaros Cathedral's interior using 3D Gaussian primitives and Gaussian Splatting, based on a set of photographs. It aims to create a detailed 3D model for generating novel views and interactive walkthroughs. Technologies include Python and C/C++, with prerequisites in visual computing and graphics courses. Expected outcomes are a Gaussian-based 3D representation and applications in scene visualization.

4000.title: VISUALIZATION LIBRARIES IN PYTHON
4000.summary: This project involves a comparative analysis of five Python data visualization libraries. The research task will be defined to evaluate library performance, usability, and features within Python development environments. The expected outcome is a detailed assessment report comparing the libraries across the defined criteria. The project requires proficiency in Python and is suited for students with background in visual computing or graphics courses. Supervision is provided by Prof. Theoharis Theoharis at NTNU.

4003.title: On the relation between software engineering and AI in Norway
4003.summary:
Research Area: Empirical study of AI-intensive system development practices in Norway.  
Technologies: AI in software engineering contexts; analysis of project reports, interviews, and software artifacts from academic-industry collaborations (TDT4290).  
Expected Outcomes: Insights into how Norwegian companies integrate AI, current trends, and implications for software engineering education and practice.

4006.title: Exploration of GraphRAG-extracted Knowledge Graphs
4006.summary: This project evaluates the value of knowledge graphs extracted by GraphRAG for downstream reasoning tasks beyond Q&A. It involves developing a tool for efficient exploration of these graphs, potentially using a graph database. Key technologies include knowledge graphs, Python/Java, and a UI framework. Expected outcome is a deeper understanding of GraphRAG's reasoning capabilities and a functional graph exploration tool.

4008.title: Bærekraftsrapportering (sustainability reporting)
4008.summary: Research area: Sustainability reporting under the EU's CSRD, focusing on scope 3 emissions data. Technologies: Data collection, processing, and reporting systems. Expected outcomes: Identify key challenges and develop practical solutions for companies like Aneo AS to comply with new regulatory requirements.

4011.title: Samarbeide og kommunikasjon i helsesektoren
4011.summary: This project investigates fragmented healthcare collaboration due to siloed information systems and professional boundaries. It focuses on implementing a digital service to enhance cross-boundary communication. Through empirical methods like observation, interviews, and system logging, it gathers and operationalizes requirements into recommended functionalities, potentially including prototypes. Expected outcomes are ICT-driven solutions to improve distributed work processes and patient care quality.

4012.title: Compilers for Approximate Computing
4012.summary: This project focuses on compiler techniques for approximate computing via precision tuning, where data types are modified to trade off numerical accuracy for gains in performance, energy efficiency, or area. You will develop code analyses and transformations within compilers like LLVM/MLIR to automatically apply these optimizations. Key technologies include C/C++, Git, CMake, and compiler intermediate representations. Expected outcomes are new compiler passes and tools that enable efficient approximate computing on target hardware. The work involves collaboration with Politecnico di Milano and University of Bologna.

4013.title: Chop my Chip! Dynamic Compilation Targets for Hardware-Software Codesign
4013.summary: This project develops dynamic compilation targets in LLVM for RISC-V custom ISAs to support early hardware design exploration. Using C/C++ and Python, it enables on-the-fly target definition and performance evaluation across ISA variants. Expected outcomes include tools to assess the impact of custom vector instructions on domain-specific applications like autonomous driving or cryptography, facilitating hardware-software co-design.

4014.title: Human-Centered AI for Responsible Digital Transformation
4014.summary: Research area: Human-centered AI and responsible digital transformation, focusing on integrating human values, social well-being, and business value. Technologies: Machine learning, robotics, IoT, or broader digital systems. Methods: Flexible—quantitative surveys, qualitative interviews, case studies, or design science/prototyping. Context: Sectors like healthcare, government, education, or business. Outcomes: Investigate stakeholder engagement, policy implications, or technical designs to ensure ethical, user-focused AI deployment.

4015.title: Unlocking Insights from Video Content – A Large Language Model Approach
4015.summary: This project develops a system to automatically retrieve and analyze YouTube video transcripts using large language models (LLMs). It focuses on extracting topics, summarizing content, and visualizing trends from aggregated playlists. Key technologies include Python, API integration, NLP, and data visualization. The expected outcome is a user-friendly tool for exploring insights from video content, validated through a user study evaluating its usability and effectiveness.

4017.title: Extending hybrid vector search (multiple projects)
4017.summary: Research Area: Extending hybrid vector search algorithms/indexes for vector databases. Technologies: C++ programming, approximate nearest neighbor search, GPU/learned indexes, database operations (joins, streaming), multi-model queries. Expected Outcomes: Develop and evaluate novel hybrid search extensions (e.g., filtering, spatio-textual, trajectory) on large datasets using pre-trained embeddings.

4018.title: [NorwAI] Exploring Meta-Learning Strategies for Few-Shot Graph Learning Tasks
4018.summary: This project investigates meta-learning strategies for few-shot learning on graph-structured data using graph neural networks (GNNs). It aims to develop and analyze algorithms that enable models to generalize to new graph tasks with minimal labeled examples by transferring knowledge across tasks. Key activities include implementing baseline methods, designing novel GNN-adapted meta-learning approaches, and creating visualization tools for meta-knowledge transfer. Expected outcomes are a comparative analysis of strategies, novel tailored algorithms, visualization techniques, and practical guidelines for applying meta-learning to real-world graph problems with scarce data.

4019.title: [NorwAI] Examining Adaptation Shifting in Graph Domain Adaptation Tasks
4019.summary: This project investigates adaptation shifting mechanisms in Graph Domain Adaptation (GDA), focusing on adaptation-based methods. It will characterize domain shifts in graph data, evaluate existing GDA methods' effectiveness against these shifts, and develop metrics and visualization tools for shift measurement. The work includes implementing state-of-the-art methods, exploring novel adaptation algorithms, and conducting comparative experiments. Expected outcomes are a comprehensive analysis of adaptation shifts, new evaluation metrics and visualization techniques, experimental insights into shift impacts, and practical guidelines for selecting and designing GDA models.

4021.title: How well do deep learning 3D object classification methods work with large datasets?
4021.summary: This project evaluates deep learning methods for 3D object classification using larger datasets instead of standard small benchmarks like ModelNet40. It aims to provide more reliable performance comparisons and insights into method scalability, addressing the issue of marginal improvements (0.1%) on limited data. Technologies include existing deep learning models and new large-scale 3D object datasets. Expected outcomes are a clearer quantitative assessment of state-of-the-art methods and guidance for future research.

4022.title: Anomaly detection w/ The National Audit Office of Norway (Riksrevisjonen)
4022.summary: This project applies unsupervised machine learning (e.g., isolation forest, autoencoders) to detect anomalies in Norwegian government financial transaction data for audit purposes. Key research involves creating explainable visualizations for auditors, testing conformance checks, deriving audit evidence from black-box models, and generating synthetic anomalies for testing. The student will use Python or R, handle potentially large datasets, and acquire financial auditing domain knowledge. Expected outcomes are practical methods that integrate advanced anomaly detection with audit standards to improve governmental accountability and fraud detection. Backup industrial data sources exist if government data is unsuitable.

4023.title: Probabilistic models for Generative AI
4023.summary: Research area: Generative AI using probabilistic models, focusing on diffusion models and alternatives like Bayesian flow networks. Technologies: Diffusion models, flow matching, comparisons with GANs/VAEs. Scope: Adaptable to theoretical analysis, comparative studies, or practical applications on custom datasets (e.g., images, video, time series, graphs). Expected outcomes: Vary by task—could include theoretical insights, empirical model evaluations, or implemented generative systems.

4024.title: [Collaboration with Maritime Robotics] Efficient Vision Transformers for Real-Time Object Segmentation and Deployment on NVIDIA AGX Orin
4024.summary: This project focuses on optimizing Vision Transformer (ViT) models for real-time object detection and segmentation on the resource-constrained NVIDIA AGX Orin platform. It will develop and implement transformer-specific techniques like token pruning, sparse attention, and GPU-DLA offloading to reduce latency and power consumption. The research compares ViT and CNN (RTMDet) optimization strategies, aiming to deploy an efficient ViT within Maritime Robotics' SeaSight system for maritime surveillance, balancing accuracy with edge deployment constraints. Tools include PyTorch, TensorRT, and profiling tools on Jetson hardware.

4025.title: [Collaboration with Maritime Robotics] Optimising RTMDet / RTMDet-Ins on NVIDIA AGX Orin
4025.summary: This project focuses on optimizing the RTMDet object detection model for real-time performance on the NVIDIA AGX Orin edge platform. Key techniques include quantization, pruning, and GPU-DLA core offloading using TensorRT. The goal is to minimize inference latency and power consumption while maintaining accuracy for maritime robotics applications. The optimized model will be integrated into the SeaSight pipeline for unmanned surface vehicle operators. Expected outcomes are an efficient RTMDet implementation and a tailored optimization framework for Orin's heterogeneous architecture.

4027.title: Employee-driven digital transformation
4027.summary: This empirical research project investigates employee participation in digital transformation, analyzing successes and failures to identify challenges and recommend best practices. You will conduct qualitative studies in organizations undergoing digitalization, focusing on how to effectively include employee knowledge and practices. The goal is to develop actionable frameworks for more inclusive and successful transformation initiatives. The specific case and focus will be defined collaboratively, requiring strong interest in qualitative research methods.

4028.title: Qualitative study of Microsoft Copilot use by computing students
4028.summary: Qualitative research study investigating how computing students at NTNU use Microsoft Copilot. The project employs observational studies, interviews, and archival data analysis to understand usage practices and their impact on learning. Expected outcomes include a rich explanatory account of student behaviors and the educational implications of generative AI tool adoption in computing education.

4029.title: [ExerGames] Exercise Bike Game Development and User Evaluation 2025/2026
4029.summary: Research Area: Exergame design integrating exercise bikes as core game controllers. Technologies: Unity game engine. Expected Outcomes: A playable prototype game using pedaling mechanics alongside button inputs, plus a user study evaluating its impact on enjoyment, motivation, and physical activity. Suitable for a two-student team with prior Unity experience.

4030.title: [ExerGames] Play to get fit 2025/2026
4030.summary: Develop exergames integrating physical exercise with engaging gameplay. Three-phase approach: research on game-based motivation, prototype development using various technologies, and evaluation for effectiveness and player engagement. Aims to balance fun and fitness in a prototype. Requires a team of two students.

4031.title: [BitPet]  AI Integration in a Location-Based AR Game for Socialization and Physical Activity (2025/2026)
4031.summary: This project explores AI integration to enhance gameplay, engagement, and socialization in BitPet, a location-based AR game promoting physical activity. Research will cover AR, location-based tech, and gaming AI. Development involves designing and implementing AI-driven features using Unity (front-end) and back-end systems. The outcome includes functional AI enhancements evaluated through real-user testing. Designed for two students; Unity experience is beneficial.

4032.title: [BitPet]  Empirical Study on the Physical and Social Health Effects of Playing BitPet (2025/2026)
4032.summary: This project empirically studies the physical and social health effects of BitPet, a location-based augmented reality game. It involves designing and conducting research via questionnaires, interviews, and in-game data analysis to assess impacts on activity, usability, and motivation. Expected outcomes include an evaluation of health benefits, identification of key game elements, design guidelines for similar exergames, and extensions to theoretical frameworks. Co-supervised by NTNU's Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences.

4033.title: A computer game to teach EU AI Act for the healthcare domain users
4033.summary: This project designs and develops an educational computer game to teach healthcare stakeholders about the complex EU AI Act, focusing on high-risk AI applications. Using design science research, the team will create a game prototype and a methodology for such educational games. Healthcare professionals will pilot and evaluate the game.

4034.title: Agentic AI security vulnerabilities identification and mitigation
4034.summary: This project addresses security vulnerabilities in Agentic AI systems that use LLMs and multiple agents, which face complex attacks like indirect prompt injection. Through systematic literature review and design science research, it aims to develop identification approaches and mitigation countermeasures. Expected outcomes include practical tools and prototypes to enhance Agentic AI security, requiring skills in AI, cybersecurity, and coding.

4039.title: RAY TRACING DATA STRUCTURES (with ARM Trondheim)
4039.summary: Master's project on enhancing Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH) construction for real-time ray tracing. Implement standard algorithms like SAH or LBVH in C++, evaluate on ARM hardware, with optional Vulkan Compute implementation. Focus on optimizing trade-offs between build time and traversal performance. Establish test pipeline for thesis to innovate in BVH methods, assessing build speed, memory usage, and framerate impact on sample content.

4042.title: Memetic and Multimethod Stochastic Optimization Algorithms
4042.summary: This project focuses on developing memetic algorithms (MAs) that integrate stochastic local search (SLS) and evolutionary algorithms (EAs) for stochastic optimization, particularly on multimodal functions. Key technologies include niching (e.g., crowding) and parameter control to balance exploration and exploitation. Expected outcomes are novel multi-method algorithms empirically tested on combinatorial problems (e.g., feature selection, SAT) with potential theoretical analysis of their performance. The goal is to improve solvers for AI/ML applications by addressing limitations in current state-of-the-art.

4043.title: Implementation and Evaluation of Scalar Evolution Analysis
4043.summary: This project implements scalar evolution analysis in the JLM compiler, which uses the Regionalized Value State Dependence Graph (RVSDG) intermediate representation instead of traditional CFG/SSA forms. You will learn the RVSDG IR and JLM's C++ codebase to build a scalar evolution pass. The analysis identifies induction variable patterns in loops to enable optimizations like strength reduction. The outcome is a implemented pass and its evaluation via loop transformations, providing relevant compiler development experience with LLVM-based tools.

4044.title: Efficient Instruction Scheduling for High-Performance Cores
4044.summary: This project explores using instruction dependencies learned during register renaming to enhance instruction scheduling in processor cores. The goal is to narrow the performance gap between simple in-order (InO) and complex out-of-order (OoO) schedulers. You will research existing scheduling techniques, implement identified improvements in the gem5 simulator (C++), and evaluate the results. Strong C++ programming skills and computer architecture knowledge are required. Familiarity with Chisel and RISC-V cores (Rocket/BOOM) is beneficial for an alternative implementation path. Expected outcome is an analysis of scheduling improvements from leveraging renaming-time dependencies.

4045.title: Explaining AI-based Recommendations (with Sparebank 1 SMN)
4045.summary: This project focuses on Explainable AI (XAI) for banking product recommendations. The candidate will investigate methods to interpret complex models like deep neural networks, addressing the need for employees to understand AI-driven suggestions. Using a large real-world dataset (~200k records, 2k variables) from Sparebank 1 SMN, the work involves applying and evaluating XAI techniques to provide clear explanations for model outputs. The expected outcome is a framework or set of methods that makes AI recommendations transparent and trustworthy for banking staff.

4046.title: Chisel for Machine Learning
4046.summary: This project extends Chisel4ml, a framework using the Chisel hardware description language to generate low-latency ML inference accelerators, targeting larger neural network models. You will analyze state-of-the-art ML hardware, compare implementations (like hls4ml), and enhance the framework's model support or target platforms. Required skills include proficiency in a hardware description language (Chisel, Verilog, VHDL) and familiarity with FPGA/ASIC design and ONNX. The outcome is a deeper understanding of ML accelerator design and an improved Chisel-based toolchain.

4047.title: Algorithmic Problems (2025)
4047.summary: Research area: Algorithms and data structures. Project types include designing new algorithms/data structures, theoretical analysis, implementation/benchmarking, or applying algorithms to new problems. Technologies depend on project focus (e.g., theoretical analysis or programming for implementation). Expected outcomes: novel algorithmic solutions, improved theoretical understanding, practical implementations, or new applications. Collaboration between two students is possible and encouraged. The scope is flexible and can be tailored to student interests, including interdisciplinary applications.

4048.title: Rare Anomaly Detection in Time Series (with SINTEF, Statnett)
4048.summary: Research Area: Time series anomaly detection for power grid infrastructure. Technologies: Machine learning/AI methods for unlabelled, noisy time series data from voltage transformers. Challenges include rare anomalies, data quality issues mimicking drift, and external dynamic factors. Expected Outcomes: Models enabling early detection of maintenance-needing deviations between three-phase voltage readings, predicting time-to-threshold, improving on current threshold-based drift detection for Statnett's 1000+ transformers.

4049.title: LLMs for Maritime language
4049.summary: This project explores applying Large Language Models (LLMs) to the maritime domain. The candidate will collect maritime documents and evaluate techniques like fine-tuning, continued pre-training, and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) to optimize LLMs for tasks such as answering questions about maritime regulations. The expected outcome is to determine the utility and best augmentation methods for LLMs in this specialized language context.

4050.title: AI-powered Data Extraction from Archival Records (with Arkivverket)
4050.summary: This project explores AI applications to improve accessibility of historical archival records, focusing on handwritten, born-digital, and pre-19th century documents. It will investigate Large Language Models (LLMs) and image recognition techniques. Conducted in collaboration with the National Archives of Norway (Arkivverket), the work involves developing and evaluating a practical solution for a selected data source, with guidance from archival experts and co-supervision by Inga Lang. The expected outcome is a demonstrable method for enhancing digital access to challenging historical materials.

4051.title: Interoperability of Learning Systems
4051.summary: This project addresses learning systems interoperability in Norway's educational ecosystem, focusing on xAPI and LTI standards to integrate data from platforms like FS and Canvas. The research involves designing and implementing a prototype system using Python/JavaScript to aggregate learning activity data via APIs and develop a visualization dashboard. The expected outcome is a tool providing teachers, students, and administrators with a unified view of cross-system learning analytics, demonstrated through real-time or batch data processing and evaluated with sample datasets.

4052.title: AI-Powered Processing of Public Consultation Responses
4052.summary: This project explores AI-powered text analysis to automate processing public consultation responses for Oslo Municipality. It will develop a proof-of-concept pipeline using fine-tuned Large Language Models to categorize, cluster, and summarize feedback. The research will compare the AI system's output quality and processing speed against current manual methods, aiming to improve efficiency, scalability, and reduce bias in handling large volumes of stakeholder input.

4053.title: Control and monitoring of robots through intuitive and natural language interactions
4053.summary: This project focuses on improving human-robot interaction for multi-robot missions using natural language. The research involves developing a Large Language Model (LLM) that converts natural language commands into formal mission specifications in PDDL (Planning Domain Definition Language). Key technologies are generative AI and AI planning. The expected outcome is a verified model for generating executable robot plans, with practical validation using robots at SINTEF's laboratory. The project addresses the challenge of ensuring plan correctness from LLM-generated instructions.

4054.title: Applying ChatGPT for Data Integration
4054.summary: Research area: Applying Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT to the challenge of data integration from heterogeneous sources. Key technologies: Prompt engineering, fine-tuning of LLMs. Expected outcomes: A proof-of-concept implementation comparing this novel LLM-based approach to traditional state-of-the-art methods, alongside an assessment of its potential benefits and associated risks. The project is co-supervised by SINTEF researchers.

4055.title: From Interview Transcription to Article Draft
4055.summary: This project explores AI-assisted journalism by fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT or Llama to automatically draft Norwegian-language articles from interview transcriptions. Using a dataset from Adressavisen, the candidate will develop and evaluate model drafts in a user study with journalists. The expected outcome is a system that generates preliminary article drafts, potentially streamlining the journalist's workflow.

4056.title: GPU adaptations of 3D shape matching algorithms
4056.summary: Research area: Adapting 3D shape matching algorithms for GPU execution to accelerate point correspondence detection. Technologies: GPU programming (e.g., CUDA), parallel computing, 3D geometry processing. Expected outcomes: Optimized GPU implementations with improved performance over CPU versions, analysis of challenges like memory bandwidth constraints and non-parallel operations, and insights for practical adoption in real-time applications.

4057.title: Secure player data management
4057.summary: This project investigates technical solutions using distributed storage and data governance to implement FIFPRO's Charter of Player Data Rights in football. Research will address threats, ensure data portability between clubs, and balance player privacy with beneficial data uses (e.g., injury prevention, development). The student will conduct a literature review, develop threat models, and create a proof-of-concept system for evaluation with end users. The goal is an affordable, secure data management solution accessible to all players, from amateurs to professionals, building on prior work.

4058.title: Simulation of artefacts caused by 3D capturing devices
4058.summary: This project addresses the need for scalable 3D shape matching benchmarks by developing algorithms to simulate artefacts from various 3D capture devices (e.g., LiDAR, photogrammetry). It will modify existing clean 3D meshes to model real-world scanner imperfections like noise, occlusion, and clutter. The outcome is a toolset for generating controlled, reproducible test data, enabling objective evaluation of matching algorithms under isolated and combined artefacts without costly physical scanning.

4059.title: Keypoint detection through destructive clustering for 3D shape recognition
4059.summary: This project develops a novel keypoint detector for 3D shape recognition in robot navigation (SLAM). It uses "destructive clustering" on an index tree of local 3D shape descriptors, dynamically removing non-unique surface patches to retain only globally distinctive keypoints. The expected outcome is a more efficient and accurate place recognition system by focusing on surfaces unique within the entire environment, potentially enabling real-time matching in large-scale 3D scans.

4060.title: Automating Compliance Processes in the Banking Industry
4060.summary: This project, in collaboration with Tietoevry Banking, aims to automate banking compliance by developing a prototype Agentic AI system. The research focuses on using AI agents to analyze compliance documents, map requirements to products/services (specifically Visa products), and generate reports. Key technologies include Natural Language Processing for document understanding and summarization, structured data storage, and a web-based UI for user access and data export. The expected outcome is a functional system prototype that improves the efficiency, accuracy, and cost-effectiveness of compliance management.

4061.title: Sustainable open source projects: evaluating sustainability practices of open source projects
4061.summary: Research area: Evaluating sustainability practices in open source software (OSS) across social, technical, organizational, environmental, and economic dimensions. Methods: Literature review, case study analysis of successful/unsuccessful projects, and qualitative data collection via interviews or surveys with OSS maintainers and contributors. Expected outcome: Actionable insights and best practices to enhance the long-term viability and resilience of open source projects.

4062.title: Eco-friendly mobile app development: developing guidelines for Creating Mobile Applications that Minimize Energy Consumption and Resource Use
4062.summary: This MSc thesis focuses on sustainable mobile app development, aiming to create guidelines for minimizing energy consumption and resource use throughout an application's lifecycle. The research involves benchmarking energy patterns in popular apps using analysis tools and conducting interviews with developers and industry experts. The expected outcome is a set of practical, actionable design and coding principles that empower developers to build applications with a reduced environmental footprint.

4064.title: How can we evaluate the extent to which a 3D shape matching algorithm is affected by differences in colour?
4064.summary: This project addresses the lack of benchmarks for evaluating 3D shape matching algorithms that incorporate colour information. It extends an existing shape-variation benchmark to assess how factors like lighting, material shininess, and translucency affect matching performance. The research involves developing a standardized evaluation framework for colour-appearance influences in 3D matching, leveraging expertise from the colour lab. Expected outcome is a novel benchmark to systematically measure algorithm robustness against realistic colour variations.

4065.title: IoT Aquarium
4065.summary: Research Area: IoT and Human-Computer Interaction, focusing on remote monitoring and control of a physical environment. Technologies: Sensors/actuators for aquaculture, web application development, IoT systems integration, and usability evaluation. Expected Outcomes: A functional freshwater aquarium prototype with a usable web interface, validated through user testing and literature review.

4066.title: IoT Garden Bed
4066.summary: Research area: IoT-enabled smart gardening. Technologies: sensor/actuator networks, web application development. Methods: literature review, prototyping (hardware/software), user testing. Outcome: A functional, remotely controllable garden bed prototype with a usable web interface, evaluated through user studies. Suitable for collaboration.

4067.title: Measuring the Coding Experience
4067.summary: This project investigates the programming learning experience using biometric sensors (EEG, eye tracking, GSR). It involves designing and conducting user studies, potentially comparing coding tasks with and without AI assistance. Students will analyze complex biometric data, which may require developing custom processing scripts or visualizations. The expected outcome is to derive insights into cognitive and emotional states during coding, contributing to programming pedagogy and tool design. Suitable for 1-2 students.

4070.title: Design for IT and Sustainability
4070.summary: This project designs a playful learning tool to enhance informatics students' understanding of IT sustainability, modifying the co-design toolkit Tiles. Students will develop a physical, online, or hybrid prototype that integrates sustainability reflection into IT design. Expected outcomes include a functional toolkit and its evaluation through design and prototyping phases.

4071.title: AI Powered Formative Assessment for Open-text Questions
4071.summary: This project develops an AI-driven formative assessment tool using Natural Language Processing to automatically analyze open-text student responses. It provides teachers with a real-time dashboard showing aggregated performance categories (correct, partially correct, incorrect) and identifying common misconceptions. The system evaluates responses for correctness, relevance, coherence, and depth. Expected outcomes include adaptive feedback for students, dynamic lecture adjustment, and a validated prototype tested in live lectures, advancing AI in education.

4073.title: Games - Games for reflection on ethical aspects of IT tools
4073.summary: This project develops and evaluates a game to promote reflection on ethical issues in IT tools, such as algorithmic bias and privacy, among informatics students. Using game-based learning, it addresses complexities from digitalization. Expected outcome: an interactive educational tool that facilitates discussion and critical thinking, building on existing group research. Technologies include game development platforms and evaluation methods.

4074.title: Agile Question Generation in Student Response Systems
4074.summary: This project develops an AI-driven system for real-time generation of follow-up questions in classroom response systems. It uses natural language processing to analyze students' open-text answers and produce contextually relevant questions during lectures. The expected outcome is a working prototype evaluated through user studies to measure its impact on student engagement, learning outcomes, and teaching adaptability. The research area is educational technology, focusing on interactive learning and automated question generation.

4075.title: Generative AI and Computational Creative Artform Transitions (e.g., Text to Images, Images to Music or Music to Text)
4075.summary: Research area: Computational creativity using generative AI to explore transitions between creative artforms (e.g., text-to-image, image-to-music, music-to-text). Technologies involve multimodal generative models like Stable Diffusion. Expected outcomes include novel AI systems that produce or evaluate creative works across domains and tools to support human creative processes.

4076.title: Discovery of program control flow in binary programs from unknown instruction set architectures
4076.summary:
Research Area: Reverse engineering binary programs with unknown or undocumented instruction set architectures (ISAs), such as custom virtual machines in malware.

Technologies: Heuristic analysis methods applied to binary code. Strong Python programming skills are desirable.

Expected Outcomes: A method to automatically recover program control flow by identifying subroutine structures (CALL/RET), branches, jumps, and entry points without prior ISA knowledge.

4077.title: Detecting Social Media Risk Users
4077.summary: This project focuses on detecting at-risk social media users for security (radicalization, predation) and mental health (depression, suicide) concerns. It involves applying machine learning and deep learning techniques to textual social media data. The work requires gathering a substantial dataset, either from existing sources or new collection. The expected outcome is the development and evaluation of predictive models for early identification of these risk categories.

4081.title: Games - Ready for digital transformation
4081.summary: This project involves designing a game to improve workers' systemic and critical thinking about digital transformation's impact and possibilities in workplaces. Students will define learning objectives, game genre, and technology with supervisor input, then proceed to design, prototype, and evaluate the game. The outcome is a functional game prototype addressing competence gaps in technology adoption.

4083.title: Games - Reflective and collaborative games for learning about creativity and AI
4083.summary: Research area: Designing a game to teach ethical and responsible AI use for supporting creativity and innovation. Technologies: Game design and development, potentially integrating AI tools (e.g., generative AI). Outcomes: A collaboratively designed game prototype and evaluation plan, focused on specific target groups and learning objectives. The project explores how AI can augment creative processes, with flexibility for students to define the game's genre, technology, and context in cooperation with the supervisor.

4084.title: IMTEL - Supporting learning with cutting-edge XR technology: Apple Vision Pro, VR treadmill and haptic suit/gloves (COPY)
4084.summary: This project explores advanced XR technologies including Apple Vision Pro, VR treadmills, and haptic suits to enhance learning at university and professional levels. Students will investigate applications such as 3D visualization, spatial computing games, walking-based simulations, and tactile feedback for educational contexts like architecture or emergency training. Access to a comprehensive VR lab is provided.

4088.title: Discovery of ISA features from binary programs from unknown instruction set architectures
4088.summary: This project focuses on reverse engineering for binaries with unknown instruction set architectures (ISAs). It requires developing a heuristic method in Python to automatically extract key ISA features—such as word size, endianness, register count, and instruction format—directly from binary code. The research addresses cases where ISAs are undocumented or custom, like in malware. Expected outcome is a tool or technique that enables disassembly without prior ISA knowledge. Strong programming skills are essential; computer architecture knowledge is beneficial but not mandatory.

4089.title: Sentiment and/or Figurative Language Analysis in Social Media
4089.summary: Research area: Natural language processing for social media, focusing on sentiment analysis and figurative language (sarcasm, irony, metaphor). Technologies: Machine learning and NLP techniques to classify sentiment polarity and intensity, while addressing challenges like negation and indirect meaning. Expected outcomes: A system that more accurately detects user sentiment in social media posts containing figurative language, demonstrating improved performance over word-based approaches.

4091.title: Identifying Online Hate Speech and Cyber Bullying
4091.summary: This project addresses the need for automated detection of online hate speech and cyberbullying to overcome slow manual moderation. It involves reviewing existing research and utilizing annotated datasets from sources like chat rooms, online games, Wikipedia, and X/Twitter. The core work is investigating and applying various machine learning methods to identify abusive language effectively. The expected outcome is an improved computational model for accurate and timely hate speech identification.

4092.title: Generativ KI og kritisk tenking
4092.summary: Empirical study investigating how generative AI tools like ChatGPT and Copilot impact critical thinking in academic knowledge work. Research involves qualitative methods including literature review, case study design, observations, interviews, and analysis of archival data. Expected outcome is a rigorous analysis of AI's influence on the ability to evaluate claims, addressing challenges like hallucination and bias. Project requires strong interest in qualitative research and can be written in English or Norwegian.

4093.title: Smidig droneutvikling
4093.summary: This project explores agile development practices for drone and AI systems within modern defense contexts. It combines a literature review on best practices with a qualitative case study involving Norwegian defense startups and drone companies. The research will use interviews, observations, and archival data. The expected outcome is an analysis of how well Norway's defense sector adapts to rapid, agile technology development, providing insights into current capabilities and challenges. The work requires an interest in empirical qualitative research.

4094.title: Multi-agent systemer i organisasjoner
4094.summary: This project explores implementing Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) within organizations to enhance collaboration between technical AI agents (including language models) and human workers. Using action-design research, you will conduct a design science study involving requirements gathering, prototype development, and evaluation via observations and interviews. The research-intensive thesis, suitable for one or two highly self-directed students, will be defined in collaboration with SINTEF Digital and industry partners. Expected outcomes include a developed MAS prototype and empirical insights into its practical effects and challenges in an organizational context.

4095.title: QDA-Flow ("Qualitative Data Analysis"-Flow): En modulær pipeline for analyse av kvalitative intervju-transkripsjoner
4095.summary: This project develops a modular Python pipeline for analyzing qualitative interview transcriptions using digital methods. It will integrate state-of-the-art techniques including NLP, thematic clustering, network analysis, and visualization. The prototype will output insights such as word clouds, frequent terms, relationship maps, sentiment, and metadata-filtered views. Following action-design research, the system will be evaluated with researchers in practice. The outcome is an extensible tool to support qualitative data analysis.

4096.title: Biologically Plausible Artificial Intelligence
4096.summary: This project explores biologically plausible local learning rules as an alternative to backpropagation, aiming to create more robust machine learning models resistant to issues like catastrophic forgetting. Building on prior work for unsupervised pattern clustering, the research will develop improved image or sound representations and investigate parallelization for specialized hardware like FPGA/ASIC. Expected outcomes include novel, robust representation methods and scalable implementations.

4097.title: Architecture-based reliability evaluation of machine learning pipelines
4097.summary: Research area: Applying architecture-based reliability modeling (e.g., Fault Trees) to machine learning pipelines to compare fault tolerance. Technologies: Machine learning, Python, TensorBoard/Netron for pipeline visualization. Expected outcome: A method to automatically derive reliability models from ML pipeline diagrams, enabling architects to evaluate and compare different ML architectures for dependability.

4098.title: Co-design of courses: Digital Competencies 4 ALL
4098.summary: The project involves co-designing digital competency courses for workplaces using a playful toolkit inspired by board games and card-based tools like Tiles. It focuses on life-long learning, aiming to engage learners in creating relevant, context-aware learning activities. Expected outcomes include the design and evaluation of an interactive toolkit for co-designing educational opportunities.

4099.title: Digital platform fairness
4099.summary: Research area: Platform fairness, power centralization, and core-periphery dynamics in digital platforms. Empirical qualitative research investigating existing platforms (e.g., Uber, Foodora) or temp agencies to generate design knowledge for fairer platforms. Technologies: Qualitative empirical methods. Outcomes: New design insights and knowledge on improving platform fairness. Thesis in English or Norwegian (English recommended).

4101.title: Competency frameworks made useful
4101.summary: This project addresses the usability gap of competency frameworks for individual professional development. It involves designing, prototyping, and evaluating a playful, cooperative, and reflective web-based tool. The prototype will be built using the "ResearchComp" framework but designed for general adaptability. Key outcomes include a functional system enabling users to self-assess competencies, compare them to framework standards, and plan development, evaluated through user testing.

4102.title: Collaboration between AI and knowledge workers
4102.summary: Research Area: Empirical study of human-AI collaboration models in knowledge work. Technologies: Various AI types, including generative AI. Methods: Empirical qualitative research (case studies, design science). Outcomes: New empirical knowledge, collaboration models, or interaction design ideas. Focus: Daily cooperation between knowledge workers and AI in organizations. Language: Norwegian or English for work and thesis. Requires interest in qualitative research; contact supervisor beforehand.

4103.title: Define your own topic in software and systems reliability
4103.summary: This open-ended master's project focuses on software and systems reliability, safety, or security. Students can propose a topic aligned with their interests, suitable for a master's level. Potential research directions include modeling reliability properties, applying formal methods like model checking, fault injection, or testing. The project's specific scope and technologies will be defined in consultation with the supervisor, aiming for a substantial research contribution in the chosen reliability domain.

4104.title: Define your own topic in software and systems modeling
4104.summary: Open-ended master's project in software and systems modeling. Students collaborate with the supervisor to define a custom topic within areas such as system architecture modeling, formal methods (e.g., model checking), Domain-Specific Languages (DSL), or metamodeling. The project's scope and specific technologies are determined through discussion, aiming for a research-oriented outcome suitable for a Master's thesis. Contact the supervisor to propose an idea.

4105.title: Defining a metamodel for concepts related to LLMs and generative AI
4105.summary: This project focuses on defining a metamodel and domain-specific language (DSL) for concepts related to Large Language Models (LLMs) and generative AI in software engineering. The goal is to create a standardized way to document GenAI components within software system architectures. Key technologies include metamodeling, DSL design, and foundational knowledge of LLMs. Expected outcomes are the metamodel, the DSL itself, and a foundation for a methodology to specify and document GenAI in architectures.

4106.title: Software architecure modeling of AI-enabled systems
4106.summary: This project investigates the limitations of traditional Architecture Description Languages (like UML, SysML) for modeling software architectures containing AI components (e.g., models, agents, prompts). Through a literature review and experimental modeling, it aims to identify these gaps. The ultimate goal is to contribute new modeling languages or patterns specifically designed for AI-enabled systems. Required skills include strong abstraction abilities and familiarity with software architecture fundamentals. Optional experience with ADLs like SysML or AADL is beneficial.

4107.title: Using LLMs for configuring static analysis tools
4107.summary: This project investigates using Large Language Models (LLMs) to configure static analysis tools (e.g., SonarQube, PMD) by translating natural language rule descriptions into tool configurations. It involves a literature review and applying open-source LLMs to datasets of existing rules, evaluating their performance. The expected outcome is to assess if LLMs can simplify the creation and customization of coding rules, potentially improving software fault detection. Required skills include machine learning and Python; experience with static analysis tools is beneficial.

4108.title: Life-long learning models for digital transformation
4108.summary: This project focuses on designing lifelong learning models for busy practitioners needing continuous skill updates in digital transformation contexts like AI and platforms. Using empirical qualitative methods (co-design, case studies), you will develop new pedagogical models and tool designs. Expected outcomes include original knowledge, practical models, and design ideas for systematic on-the-job learning. Requires strong interest in empirical qualitative research; thesis recommended in English. Contact supervisor before applying.

4111.title: Fake News/Disinformation Detection
4111.summary: Research area: Fake news and disinformation detection. Technologies: Machine learning, natural language processing, semantic web. Methods involve analyzing and tracking news from diverse sources. Expected outcome: An efficient system or approach for identifying fake news, contributing to misinformation mitigation.

4112.title: Characteristics of successful AI-Adoption in large organizations: OsloKommune
4112.summary: Research area: AI adoption in the public sector, focusing on Oslo Municipality. Technologies: Analysis of the municipality's "AI factory" (a reusable module system for AI solutions). Methods: Qualitative research (interviews, observations, document analysis). Expected outcomes: Identify success/failure factors for impactful AI adoption, develop a tailored adoption model or toolbox, produce analysis and recommendations for the AI factory, and inform best practices for sustainable municipal AI implementation.

4114.title: Mapping future applications of responsible AI: OsloKommune
4114.summary: This project maps current and future AI use cases within Oslo Municipality through a responsible AI lens. Using qualitative methods (interviews, analysis), students will review existing applications, engage stakeholders, and develop a strategic roadmap emphasizing ethics, explainability, and transparency. The work involves analyzing the municipality's new AI factory, providing recommendations for improvement, and creating a structured overview to inform long-term AI strategy and public value prioritization in citizen-facing services.

4116.title: Trust and quality in AI-generated map data (Explainable AI): Kartverket
4116.summary: This project investigates how Explainable AI (XAI) can enhance trust in AI-generated map analytics within the public sector. Using a case study with Kartverket (the Norwegian Mapping Authority), it adopts a sociotechnical perspective to examine reliability and verifiability in automated spatial data analysis and visualization. The research aims to develop practical guidelines for implementing trustworthy XAI in public data services, addressing quality assurance and credibility challenges. Technologies include AI for geospatial analysis and XAI methods, with outcomes focused on framework development for public sector AI deployments.

4120.title: Scalable multi-modal vector search
4120.summary: Research area: Scalable approximate nearest neighbor search for multi-modal vector data (e.g., image-text pairs). Technologies: DiskANN-inspired algorithms, C++ implementation. Expected outcomes: Develop or extend indexing solutions to handle datasets larger than current state-of-the-art, evaluate on large-scale datasets. Focus is on algorithmic/indexing improvements using pre-trained embeddings, not embedding creation. Prerequisites: Strong C++ skills and interest in algorithms.

4121.title: *NEW*: AI-Driven Multi-Modal Perception for Autonomous Systems Using LiDAR and Stereo Vision (2025-2026)
4121.summary: This project focuses on AI-driven sensor fusion, combining LiDAR and stereo vision data to enhance perception for autonomous systems. Key technologies include deep learning for multi-modal data fusion. Expected outcomes are improved environmental understanding, leading to more robust navigation, obstacle detection, and object recognition. The research is applicable to various mobile robotic platforms, from maritime vessels to racing cars and mini-robots, within an interdisciplinary setting emphasizing real-world autonomy challenges.

4127.title: *NEW*:   AI Projects related to Salmon Health Tracking  (7 different projects)
4127.summary: This cluster offers seven MSc projects applying AI and computer vision to automate salmon welfare monitoring in aquaculture. Research areas include unsupervised welfare indicator detection，stereo vision for semantic segmentation，behavioral analysis，automated annotation，individual fish re-identification，3D model construction from monocular video，and pose/size estimation from stereo images. Core technologies are deep learning，3D reconstruction，and semantic segmentation. Expected outcomes are automated systems for real-time health assessment，individual tracking，growth monitoring，and scalable data annotation tools，validated in real aquaculture environments to improve fish welfare and production efficiency.

4128.title: *NEW*:  Effective Sample Selection for Training Maritime Object Detection Models  (coop. with Maritime Robotics)
4128.summary: This project develops sample selection strategies to reduce annotation costs for training maritime object detection models on Unmanned Surface Vessels (USVs). It will investigate algorithms using domain knowledge (e.g., AIS, GPS) and Active Learning. A key focus is implementing and evaluating online (real-time on the USV) versus offline (central server) approaches. The expected outcome is an algorithm that selects the most informative samples, improving training efficiency compared to random sampling for the Maritime Robotics Otter USV.

4129.title: *NEW*:  Instance Segmentation for Unmanned Surface Vessels Fusing LIDAR and Camera  (in coop. with Maritime Robotics)
4129.summary: This project explores LIDAR-camera fusion for 2D instance segmentation to enhance Unmanned Surface Vessel perception in near-shore environments. Using data from Maritime Robotics' SeaSight system, it reprojects LIDAR point clouds into the camera frame and integrates them with RGB images in a YOLO-based model. The research evaluates fusion techniques, quantifies improvements over single-sensor methods, and validates the integrated model experimentally, aiming for robust obstacle detection and navigation safety.

4130.title: *NEW* Temporal-YOLO:   Making object detectors stable and reliable
4130.summary: Research focuses on developing a temporally stable version of the YOLO object detector for video streams. The project combines deep learning (YOLO) with classical detection theory and temporal statistical processes to eliminate flickering and duplicate detections. Expected outcomes include a model producing smooth, reliable sequences of bounding boxes and segmentation masks by systematically incorporating detection history. Suited for students proficient in deep learning and stochastic processes mathematics.

4131.title: Generative AI and solving math word problems
4131.summary: This project explores using Generative AI, specifically transformer-based large language models (GPT, LLaMA, Mistral), to solve mathematical word problems. The candidate will curate datasets, fine-tune a model, and evaluate its accuracy and reasoning against traditional rule-based methods. Expected outcomes include a comparative analysis of AI performance and insights into educational applications. Requires Python/Java skills, basic machine learning knowledge, and interest in hands-on development.

4132.title: Generative AI and solving visual math problems
4132.summary: Research Area: Applying Generative AI to solve visual mathematical problems (graphs, diagrams, handwritten equations). Technologies: Multimodal AI (text + image), model fine-tuning, Python/Java programming. Expected Outcomes: Development of AI models for visual math interpretation, evaluation of their reasoning and generalization across problem types, and exploration of applications in education and automated assessment.

4133.title: Generative AI and Usability testing
4133.summary: Research area: Applying Generative AI to software usability testing and UX evaluation. Technologies: Python/Java programming, machine learning, empirical user studies. Expected outcomes: Compare AI-generated usability reports and recommendations against traditional methods (heuristic evaluation, user testing), assess user perceptions of AI insights, and identify challenges/limitations of GenAI in this context.

4134.title: Generative AI and Code generation from legacy diagrams
4134.summary: Research area: Automating conversion of legacy software diagrams (UML, flowcharts) to functional code using Generative AI. Technologies: Large language/multimodal models (GPT-4, LLaVA), intermediate representations (JSON, AST). Work: Build a conversion pipeline, fine-tune models for diagram understanding, conduct usability and accuracy testing with developers. Outcome: Evaluated system demonstrating reduced effort for software modernization, with code quality assessed against manual implementations. Requires Python/Java skills and background in system design.

4135.title: Generative AI and Domain Specific Languages
4135.summary: This project explores integrating Generative AI (LLMs like GPT-4, Code Llama) into the lifecycle of Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs such as SQL, Verilog). Key technologies involve fine-tuning/prompt engineering for DSL code synthesis and debugging. Expected outcomes include a framework for AI-DSL integration, evaluation of AI-generated code correctness and efficiency, analysis of usability improvements, guidelines for AI-friendly DSL design, and a prototype AI-assisted development tool. Requirements: system design background, basic ML knowledge, strong Python/Java skills.

4136.title: Generative AI and green web design
4136.summary: This project explores using Generative AI (like GPT-4, DALL·E) to automate eco-friendly web design, aiming to reduce energy consumption and carbon footprint. Key technologies include Python/Java programming and performance tools (Lighthouse, Carbon Calculator). The candidate will develop and test AI-generated web components, comparing their efficiency, load times, and usability against traditionally built sites through quantitative analysis and user testing. The outcome is an evaluation of AI's role in creating sustainable, performant websites while balancing usability and aesthetic trade-offs.

4137.title: Generative AI and green coding
4137.summary: This project explores using Generative AI to assist in writing energy-efficient "green" code. The research involves developing or analyzing an AI-powered assistant (using models like GPT or CodeT5) to automatically generate, refactor, and optimize code. Key outcomes include assessing the AI's effectiveness by comparing the energy consumption of its code against human-written code through tools like Intel VTune, and conducting usability studies with developers to evaluate the integration of such tools into sustainable software workflows. The goal is to provide best practices for AI-assisted green coding.

4138.title: AI, Biometrics and Extreme programming
4138.summary: This research uses AI and biometric sensors (eye-tracking, EEG, facial expression) to analyze cognitive and collaborative processes in extreme programming (e.g., pair programming). It aims to identify features of successful sessions via explainable AI (XAI) and may design a real-time feedback tool for students. The project involves experimental data collection, pipeline development, and evaluation, requiring Python/Java skills and an interest in empirical software engineering.

4139.title: AI, eye-tracking and Understanding teachers
4139.summary: This project explores teacher cognition using AI-enhanced eye-tracking. The research will analyze gaze patterns of novice and experienced teachers in simulated or real classrooms to understand attention distribution, decision-making, and cognitive load. Key technologies include eye-tracking hardware, Python/Java programming, and machine learning for gaze data analysis (fixations, heatmaps). Expected outcomes are insights into teacher behavior, predictive models of instructional choices, and foundations for adaptive AI classroom support tools. Requirements: system design background, basic ML, and strong programming skills.

4140.title: Mental Health and wellbeing in Computing Education
4140.summary: This project investigates mental health challenges faced by computing students, such as stress and imposter syndrome, and evaluates support strategies. Research will use surveys, interviews, and case studies to assess impacts and existing systems. The candidate will require Python/Java programming skills and basic machine learning knowledge, potentially developing interventions like AI chatbots or curriculum changes. The goal is to propose effective wellbeing support methods for computing education.

4141.title: Improving health literacy using gamification
4141.summary: This project explores gamification to enhance health literacy by designing and evaluating a gamified health education application, such as a mobile or web-based tool. It incorporates elements like points, quests, storytelling, and feedback, developed using Python or Java. Through usability testing—including think-aloud protocols and eye-tracking—with participants, it measures engagement, knowledge retention, and satisfaction compared to traditional methods. Expected outcomes are an assessed impact on health literacy improvement and key design principles for future gamified health education tools.

4142.title: A Multimodal (Video and Audio) Approach to Developing a Recommender System using Content Narrative of Online Streamed Video Games (YouTube/Twitch)
4142.summary: This project develops a multimodal machine learning system to automatically analyze and segment gaming livestreams (YouTube/Twitch) by emotional and thematic content (e.g., exploration, combat). It uses computer vision for visual scene analysis and NLP/audio processing for emotional tone, integrating both via fusion models and temporal sequence models (LSTMs/Transformers). The expected outcome is a recommender system that assigns narrative percentages to different content types within a video and provides an overall content breakdown.

4143.title: AD - AI-agents trained end-to-end by Imitation and Reinforcement learning in simulated environments (2025)
4143.summary: This project trains end-to-end AI agents for autonomous driving using imitation and reinforcement learning in simulated environments. Key technologies include deep reinforcement learning and simulators such as CARLA, NVIDIA Omniverse, and MARS, with a focus on creating a digital twin of the local campus. Expected outcomes are robust, realistic agent training aligned with state-of-the-art methods like UniAD and TransFuser, leveraging industrial insights from Comma and Wayve.

4144.title: AD (Autonomous Driving), AI/ML/DL and Computer Vision (CV) (2025)
4144.summary: This project offers master's theses in autonomous driving (AD) research, covering end-to-end (imitation/reinforcement learning) and modular architectures (perception, planning, control). Key technologies include NVIDIA DRIVE software, ROS integration, simulation (CARLA, DRIVE Sim, NeRF), and domain adaptation. Specific topics involve point cloud processing (LiDAR, SLAM), 5G remote fleet control, and AD in Nordic winter conditions. The expected outcome is advancing AD systems for real-world deployment, including sensor-based road object assessment for Digital Road Twins.

4145.title: AI-lab pitched projects - Master-project Safari (2025)
4145.summary: This master's project proposal showcases diverse AI applications: football analytics, medical image analysis for Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm surgery decisions and brain segmentation, geospatial AI for laser point cloud classification and GNSS quality prediction, satellite-based environmental monitoring, health data digital twins, fish location prediction, and monocular depth estimation for maritime robotics. Technologies include computer vision, machine learning, and data streaming, aiming to improve decision-making, resource management, and automation across healthcare, environment, sports, and maritime sectors.

4146.title: Autonomous Driving (AD): NAP-lab related projects based on Visual Intelligence (AI/CV)++ (2025)
4146.summary: This project focuses on advancing autonomous driving through visual intelligence and AI. Key research areas include end-to-end learning (IL/RL), neural rendering for sim-to-real transfer, teleoperation (TeleDrive), HD-maps/Digital Twins (using Omniverse), control algorithms, and crowdsourced map updates. The platform features a sensor-equipped KIA e-Niro (NVIDIA DRIVE AGX Orin, cameras, LiDAR) and simulators like CARLA. Projects span simulation to real-world testing. AI/CV skills are beneficial, but other specializations are welcome. Expected outcomes include publishable research and pathways to PhD studies.

4147.title: Digital Twins (DTs) for Health and Infrastructure++ (2025)
4147.summary: This project explores advanced applications of Digital Twins (DTs) in two domains: 1) Health, for personalized self-management and clinical decision support, and 2) Road infrastructure, for monitoring and optimization. It involves developing or integrating simulation models, real-time data streams (IoT), and AI/analytics to create dynamic virtual replicas. Expected outcomes include prototype DT frameworks, case studies demonstrating utility in each field, and insights into cross-domain DT challenges and opportunities. The focus is on practical implementation and evaluating the impact of DT technology.

4148.title: Driving with 3DGS/NeRFs: Simulating sensor inputs for autonomous driving agents (2025)
4148.summary: This project addresses the lack of closed-loop camera simulation in the nuPlan driving simulator. It explores integrating novel view synthesis methods (NeRFs, 3D Gaussian Splatting) to generate on-demand, high-quality camera inputs from nuPlan's scene data. Research questions investigate what scene data is useful, the synthesis quality and speed, and the utility of these synthetic views for training and evaluating end-to-end autonomous driving systems. The expected outcome is an extended nuPlan simulator capable of closed-loop camera synthesis and an assessment of its effectiveness for AD agent development.

4149.title: Industry (and AI-lab pitched) project proposals related to Visual intelligence (AI/CV) (2025)
4149.summary: This proposal aggregates multiple industry projects in visual intelligence (AI/computer vision). Research focuses on applying deep learning to diverse real-world problems: sports analytics, medical imaging (coronary artery labeling), autonomous driving (Nordic conditions, HD-maps), industrial inspection (fouling, drone detection, aluminium grain analysis), and food processing (lobster grading). Key technologies include video analysis, LiDAR segmentation, and object detection/tracking. Expected outcomes are automated analysis tools, enhanced diagnostic support, navigation systems for automated vehicles, and quality control solutions, often with potential for employment.

4150.title: MIC - Medical Image Computing / Analysis and AI/CV (most organs / most modalities) (2025)
4150.summary: This master's project focuses on Medical Image Computing (MIC) using Deep Learning and Computer Vision. Research involves analyzing most organs (heart, brain, liver, etc.) across most imaging modalities (CT, MR, Ultrasound, pathology). Key technologies include Vision Transformers (ViT) and the MONAI framework. Projects may address challenges from conferences like MICCAI or involve model development. The work is conducted in collaboration with NTNU, SINTEF Health, and St. Olav Hospital, with outcomes including developed AI models for medical image analysis. Students can work individually or in pairs.

4151.title: 3DGS / NeRFs - photorealistic Reconstruction of complex 3D scenes (2025)
4151.summary: Project on photorealistic 3D scene reconstruction using NeRFs and related technologies like Instant-NeRF and SDF Studio. Focus on real-time applications in autonomous driving simulators (e.g., DRIVE Sim) and VR/XR. Students will experiment with these frameworks to advance reconstruction of complex scenes.

4152.title: Omniverse, Content generation, Simulation and Digital Twins (2025)
4152.summary: This project focuses on visual computing and AI for autonomous driving research. Key tasks include creating a scalable, real-time photorealistic digital twin of the Gløs campus using NVIDIA Omniverse (USD) or game engines, integrating existing geospatial data and real-time updates. It involves automating 3D content generation from 2D sources, importing the environment into simulators (DRIVE Sim, Isaac Sim, CARLA), and training autonomous driving agents via imitation and reinforcement learning. The final outcome is a fine-tuned AI model applied to a real autonomous vehicle platform for AD research.

4153.title: Óne citizen - óne digital health twin (an ecosystem of smart digital twins - towards a data-driven health service) (2025)
4153.summary: This project focuses on developing a "digital health twin" ecosystem for MIA Health, expanding beyond current activity tracking.
