Key research areas include integrating wearable data (activity, sleep), system architecture for predictive modeling, and potentially incorporating fine-tuned LLMs. Technologies involve cloud platforms, data integration, and AI. Expected outcomes are a more comprehensive health platform with predictive capabilities, tools for digital clinical studies, and robust privacy/security for sensitive health data.

4154.title: Open Foundation Models / LLM (type ChatGPT), fine-tuned and adapted to Health and integrated in a Digital Twin setting (2025)
4154.summary:
Research Area: Adapting open-source Foundation Models/LLMs for personalized health applications within a Digital Twin framework.  
Technologies: Fine-tuning open LLMs with limited data, integrating wearable health data, natural language conversation, explainable AI.  
Expected Outcomes: A personalized health-focused LLM integrated into a digital twin for conversational support, with analysis of privacy, security, and ethical challenges. Project scope adaptable to student interests.

4155.title: Virtual, Augmented and Mixed Reality for learning AI etc. (2025)
4155.summary: This project explores the use of Extended Reality (XR) technologies—Virtual, Augmented, and Mixed Reality—to create immersive learning and training environments. The research focuses on two primary application domains: 1) education in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning concepts, and 2) professional training through simulated medical cases. The expected outcome is the development and evaluation of XR-based tools that enhance understanding of complex AI topics and provide safe, practical hands-on experience for specialized professional training.

4156.title: Vision Mamba for Advanced Medical Image Analysis (2025)
4156.summary: Research Area: Medical image analysis using Vision Mamba (ViM), a State-Space Model architecture. Technologies: ViM, comparing performance against CNNs and Vision Transformers (ViT). Expected Outcomes: Theoretical analysis of ViM's advantages, practical implementation and benchmarking on medical datasets, and potential development of novel ViM architectures. Project balances theory and practice based on student interest.

4157.title: Vision Transformers (for Visual Intelligence) (2025)
4157.summary: This project focuses on Vision Transformers (ViT), exploring their theory and practical application in computer vision. You will study Transformer architectures and self-attention mechanisms, analyzing recent works. The practical component involves implementing ViTs for domains like autonomous vehicles, robotics, or medical image analysis (or a student-proposed area). The balance between theory and practice is flexible. Expected outcomes are a deep theoretical understanding of ViTs and a hands-on implementation project applying them to a chosen visual intelligence task. Foundational references include the original Transformer and ViT papers.

4158.title: Visual Creativity / Generative Visual AI / multi-modal foundation models (2025)
4158.summary: Research Area: Generative AI and multi-modal foundation models for creating images, video, 3D content, and potentially audio. Technologies: Investigation of state-of-the-art text-to-media architectures (e.g., diffusion, transformer-based models like DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, Sora). Expected Outcomes: Gain deep understanding of current generative visual AI systems and collaboratively design/implement a concrete project applying these technologies to a specific application domain.

4159.title: Visual Intelligence and accurate geo-referencing of road objects on mobile devices (2025)
4159.summary: Develop a mobile app mounted on public transport to use AI for assessing and accurately geo-referencing the condition of visible road objects. The system integrates a device camera, low-cost cm-accurate positioning (u-blox with CPos), and AI models. Expected outcomes include a functional prototype for real-time road object analysis, with applications in HD-map creation, localization, and neural rendering data collection. This collaborative project with national road authorities, SINTEF, and municipalities has significant innovation potential.

4160.title: Implementation and Evaluation of Partial Redundancy Elimination
4160.summary: This project implements a partial redundancy elimination (PRE) transformation in the JLM compiler, which uses the Regionalized Value State Dependence Graph (RVSDG) intermediate representation. The work involves researching PRE and global value numbering, adding the pass to the C++ JLM codebase (built on LLVM), and evaluating its performance against existing optimizations. The expected outcome is a functional PRE implementation and an analysis of its effectiveness within the RVSDG framework.

4161.title: *NEW*: Attention mechanisms to improve point cloud generation from images of stereo cameras in the marine domain
4161.summary: This project develops attention mechanisms for deep neural networks to improve stereo camera point cloud generation in marine environments. It addresses challenges like lack of landmarks and unreliable sky/water features by focusing on collision-relevant obstacles. The research uses simulated and multi-modal sensor data (e.g., lidar) for training, with validation via real-world tests on Fugro's USV. Expected outcomes include a robust model for marine stereo vision and practical validation data. Prerequisites: machine learning/computer vision courses, strong Python/C++ skills.

4162.title: *NEW*:  Assess weather and sea conditions from SITAW data
4162.summary: This project addresses improving situational awareness for autonomous vessels in adverse weather. It develops weather assessment methods using sensor data from cameras, LIDAR, radar, and IMU, along with forecasts, to adapt target tracking and sensor fusion algorithms. Technologies include machine learning, computer vision, and programming in Python/C++. Work is primarily simulated, with real data collected from Fugro's USV for evaluation.

4163.title: *NEW*:  Target detection and tracking using W-Band radarBackground: While the LIDAR sensor has proven its efficiency for collision avoidance and docking use cases in harbors and inland water areas, its sensitivity to adverse weather conditions such as fog
4163.summary: This project develops target detection and tracking algorithms for W-band radar (77–81 GHz) to improve maritime situational awareness, addressing limitations of LIDAR and X-band radar in offshore conditions. The research focuses on fusing W-band data with cameras, AIS, and X-band radar using sensor fusion techniques. Algorithms will be implemented in Python or C++ and validated through real-world field tests on Fugro’s unmanned surface vessel (USV). Expected outcomes include robust, all-weather tracking solutions for autonomous marine navigation near offshore structures.

4164.title: Non-visual interaction for mixed reality environments for children
4164.summary: This project explores non-visual mixed reality interaction for children, moving beyond screens and head-mounted displays. It utilizes multisensory technologies like haptics, audio, motion/gesture recognition (sensors, NFC/RFID, speech), and generative AI. Through interaction design experiments and elicitation studies, the project aims to identify optimal sensory combinations and adaptive solutions. The expected outcomes are to enable social, inclusive group play and learning while maintaining connection to the physical environment, supporting richer user experiences through embodiment.

4165.title: NeRFs in Motion: Learning Dynamic Environment Representations for Autonomous Agents
4165.summary: This project develops a dynamic Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) system for autonomous agents in unstructured environments. It fuses multi-modal sensor data—stereo vision, LiDAR, and IMU—to build real-time 3D scene representations that handle moving objects. The approach integrates visual-inertial odometry, uses dynamic NeRF variants, and segments dynamic elements. Expected outcomes include a prototype system, analysis of fidelity versus computational trade-offs, and evaluation on datasets like KITTI or Waymo for navigation tasks.

4166.title: AI as a co-teacher
4166.summary: This project explores AI as a co-teacher, developing a domain-specific LLM tool to assist teachers with course planning, assessment creation, and evaluating student learning outcomes from a course description. Using generative AI and pre-trained LLMs, the research focuses on ethical deployment to improve curricula alignment and support teacher workflows. Required skills include Python programming, machine learning, and generative AI knowledge. Expected outcomes are a functional tool that enhances educational tasks and curriculum design.

4167.title: *NEW*:  Adaptive Motion Planning for Small-Scale Autonomous Ships Using Reinforcement Learning and Model Predictive Control
4167.summary: This project develops a hybrid motion planning framework for small autonomous surface vessels in dynamic environments. It combines Reinforcement Learning (RL) with Model Predictive Control (MPC) to generate adaptive, safe trajectories. The RL-based Action Sequence Generator proposes control sequences from environmental representations (e.g., occupancy grids), which MPC validates and refines for dynamic feasibility and optimality. Key outcomes include the integrated RL-MPC architecture, an empirical analysis of environment encodings, and a simulation benchmark against baseline planners. Requires Python, basic control theory, and machine learning experience (preferably RL). Tools: PyTorch/TensorFlow, ROS2.

4168.title: RAG for Course Preparation
4168.summary: This project evaluates Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) using state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs) on domain-specific educational content for course preparation. The student will test various off-the-shelf models on benchmark datasets and a provided real-case scenario, using Python. Evaluation includes objective metrics and subjective student feedback via surveys to measure tool effectiveness. Key technologies are LLMs, RAG frameworks, and Python programming. Expected outcomes are a comparative analysis of model performance and insights into the practical utility of such a system in an educational context.

4169.title: *NEW:   SLAM for autonomous docking  (coop. with Zeabuz)
4169.summary: This project develops a robust SLAM system using LiDAR, radar, and camera data for autonomous maritime vessel docking, in collaboration with Zeabuz and NTNU. The work involves implementing and refining sensor-fusion algorithms, initially in simulation using ROS/ROS2, followed by real-world testing on NTNU’s ferry. Key outcomes include improved near-shore localization, accurate dock position estimation, and a validated solution for redundancy in GNSS-denied environments. The candidate requires strong programming (Python/C++) and SLAM/sensor fusion knowledge.

4170.title: *NEW*: Monocular depth estimation for maritime situational awareness  (coop. with Zeabuz)
4170.summary: This project develops monocular depth estimation using neural networks for maritime situational awareness. It involves creating a vision-based depth sensing pipeline, integrating it with an existing autonomy system, and benchmarking performance against radar and lidar. The focus is on adapting relative depth models scaled by radar/AIS data for effective maritime target tracking. Real-world experiments on autonomous ferries and workboats, facilitated by Zeabuz and NTNU, are expected, culminating in closed-loop collision avoidance testing.

4171.title: *NEW*:   Enabling deep learning in maritime object tracking  (coop. with Zeabuz)
4171.summary: This master's project with Zeabuz addresses maritime object tracking using deep learning. It involves developing a trigger-based system to efficiently collect relevant camera and radar data, guided by a radar tracker to avoid recording empty scenes. The master's thesis will then adapt automotive attention-based tracking methods (e.g., TrackFormer) to maritime data, using semi-supervised training. Key technologies include sensor fusion, deep learning models, and efficient data pipelines. Expected outcomes are a functional data collection system and a trained deep learning tracking model tailored for maritime environments.

4172.title: Graphical user interfaces for operating drones for ship inspections
4172.summary: This project designs and evaluates user-friendly graphical interfaces (GUIs) for operating drones during ship inspections prior to dismantling. The research focuses on human-drone interaction for non-expert users to improve safety by identifying hazardous materials in hard-to-reach areas. Key activities include studying current inspection practices, prototyping GUIs, and conducting end-user evaluations. The work is part of the SHEREC project, aiming to enhance ship-breaking safety through robotics and digitalization, with co-supervision from NTNU's Department of Design.

4173.title: Graphical user interfaces for operating magnetic crawler robots for cutting ship hulls
4173.summary: This project focuses on developing graphical user interfaces (GUIs) for operating magnetic crawler robots to cut ship hulls, aiming to improve safety in ship dismantling by replacing hazardous manual work. Key activities include researching hull-cutting processes, designing and prototyping intuitive GUIs for non-robotic experts, and evaluating these prototypes with end-users. The work is part of the SHEREC project, emphasizing digitalization and robotics in ship-breaking, with co-supervision from NTNU's Department of Design. Expected outcomes are user-friendly interfaces enabling effective remote operation of magnetic crawlers for hull cutting.

4174.title: Graphical user interfaces for operating mobile robotic arms for cutting ships internally
4174.summary: This project focuses on designing and evaluating graphical user interfaces (GUIs) for operating mobile robotic arms to cut internal ship parts during dismantling. The goal is to create user-friendly interfaces for non-robotic experts, improving safety by removing workers from hazardous cutting areas. Key activities include understanding ship cutting processes, prototyping GUIs for robotic arm control, and evaluating these prototypes with end-users. The work supports the SHEREC project's aim of digitalizing and robotizing ship-breaking for enhanced safety.

4176.title: Designing user interfaces for facilitating coordination between on-board and shore-based operators
4176.summary: This project designs and evaluates user interfaces to enhance coordination between on-board and shore-based maritime operators for reducing energy consumption. Research involves studying current communication, prototyping interfaces with environmental feedback, and testing with stakeholders. Expected outcomes include effective UI tools that improve collaboration and decision-making, contributing to lower carbon emissions in the maritime sector as part of the OpenZero project.

4177.title: User interfaces for distributed route planning and oversight for hybrid sail ships
4177.summary: This project designs and evaluates user interfaces for route planning and oversight of hybrid sail ships, integrating real-time weather forecasts and operational constraints. The research area combines maritime operations, sustainable transport, and human-computer interaction. Key technologies involve UI/UX prototyping and maritime data integration. Expected outcomes are functional interface prototypes tested with end-users to improve decision-making for wind-assisted shipping, contributing to the OpenZero project's goal of reducing maritime carbon emissions.

4178.title: Designing user interfaces for remotely operated offshore cranes
4178.summary: Research area: User interface design for remotely operated offshore cranes to enhance safety. Technologies: UI prototyping and user-centered evaluation methods. Expected outcomes: A set of user interface prototypes for remote crane operation, evaluated for effectiveness with end users, contributing to the OpenRemote project's open-source design systems for remote machinery.

4179.title: Designing a design system for operating bipedal and quadrupedal robots
4179.summary: This project focuses on developing a design system—a collection of reusable UI components and guidelines—specifically for graphical user interfaces (GUIs) that operate bipedal and quadrupedal robots. The research involves reviewing existing robot control interfaces, designing and prototyping UI elements, and evaluating them for user comprehension. As part of the open-source OpenRemote initiative, the expected outcome is a documented design system to improve consistency and efficiency in developing remote operation software for advanced robots. The project is co-supervised by Dr. Taufik Akbar Sitompul (NTNU Department of Design).

4180.title: Reuse Made Easier: Co-Designing a Digital Solution for Increased Furniture Reuse at NTNU
4180.summary: This project explores co-designing a digital solution to enhance NTNU's furniture reuse service (Gjenbrukstorget), addressing sustainability in a circular economy. Using service and participatory design, it will investigate user experiences and organizational barriers through workshops and prototyping with staff, students, and administration. The expected outcome is a user-friendly digital platform that improves coordination, accessibility, and participation, thereby reducing furniture waste and fostering sustainable practices at the university.

4181.title: MIC - Medical Image Computing / Analysis and AI/CV (most organs / most modalities) (2025)
4181.summary: Master's project in Medical Image Computing (MIC) applying deep learning and computer vision to analyze various organs (heart, liver, brain, etc.) and imaging modalities (CT, MR, Ultrasound, digital pathology). Focus on model development, e.g., U-mamba, using MONAI and MONAI Label frameworks. Collaboration with MIRA, SINTEF Health, NTNU, and St Olav Hospital. Opportunities for publication, extensive PhD support, and flexible team structures (individual or pairs). Supervised by Frank Lindseth and Gabriel Kiss.

4182.title: Autonomous Driving (AD): NAP-lab related projects based on Visual Intelligence (AI/CV)++ (2025)
4182.summary: This project focuses on advancing autonomous driving through visual intelligence and AI/computer vision. Key research areas include end-to-end driving (imitation/reinforcement learning), reducing the simulation-to-reality gap using neural reconstruction, teleoperation via 5G, developing HD-maps/digital twins for Nordic winters, crowdsourced map updates, and automated data anonymization. The NAP-lab provides a sensor-rich KIA e-Niro research vehicle, CARLA/NVIDIA DRIVE Sim for simulation, and NVIDIA Omniverse for digital twins. Projects involve both simulated and real-world testing, with opportunities for publication and potential PhD continuation.

4183.title: High quality interactive digital twins in VR
4183.summary: Master's project on developing high-quality interactive VR environments for digital twins, in collaboration with Prof. Adil Rasheed from the Institute of Cybernetics. Focuses on creating VR interfaces to visualize and interact with virtual representations of physical systems, such as autonomous aquariums, greenhouses, soil movement experiments, and electric cable overload prevention. Expected outcome: an immersive VR tool enabling real-time monitoring, control, and informed decision-making through advanced simulation and data integration.

4184.title: Agile transformation in organizations
4184.summary: Research area: Agile approaches to organizational digital transformation, contrasting with high-risk "big bang" methods. Methods: Qualitative research via literature review and a real-world case study with interviews/observations. Technologies: Focuses on organizational practices and methodologies, not specific tech. Expected outcomes: Analysis of successful agile transformation practices, generation of new knowledge, and actionable recommendations for organizations. Thesis language: Norwegian or English (English recommended). Requires interest in empirical qualitative research.

4185.title: Combining EEG and motion capture in immersive VR environment (2025)
4185.summary: This project integrates EEG and machine learning-based human pose estimation (HPE) within a VR exergame for rehabilitation. The research focuses on synchronizing brain activity data with movement tracking to provide holistic feedback on cognitive states like attention and movement intention. Key technologies include EEG, HPE, VR, and data synchronization pipelines like NeuroPype/Timeflux using LabStreamingLayer. The expected outcome is a functional, time-synchronized pipeline enabling combined analysis of neural and motor data to enhance rehabilitation progress.

4186.title: Sensorimplementering i skipsbransjen og behandling av skipsdata
4186.summary: This project investigates sensor-based automation of ship classification to reduce manual inspections and enable continuous monitoring. Key challenges include data sharing, standardizing heterogeneous formats, and scaling the VISTA Gateway system. It will explore combining standards (e.g., integrating non-ISO MAD data) and optimizing real-time versus batch processing. Expected outcomes assess technical requirements for data storage/processing, organizational impacts (internal/external), and the sustainability effects of sensor implementation.

4187.title: Chatbots in public sector
4187.summary: Research area: Use and inclusivity of public sector chatbots in Norway. Methods: Literature analysis followed by empirical qualitative study (e.g., interviews, observations) on citizen interactions. Technologies: Chatbot platforms, qualitative research methods. Expected outcomes: Empirical knowledge on citizen usage and recommendations for developing inclusive, effective public sector chatbots. Working language: Norwegian or English (thesis recommended in English). Requires interest in empirical qualitative research. Contact supervisor before selecting.

4188.title: Performance study of parallelization techniques for proxy applications in scientific computing
4188.summary: This project studies parallelization techniques for proxy applications in scientific computing to enhance performance on HPC architectures. Students will choose a specific proxy application and methods with supervisor input. Expected outcomes include optimized parallel performance and insights into effective techniques for scientific computing bottlenecks.

4189.title: Profiling submissions for AI assisted content
4189.summary:
Research Area: Academic integrity and AI-assisted content detection in student submissions.

Technologies: Deep learning, specifically attention-based models, to analyze writing style patterns.

Expected Outcomes: A system for profiling submissions to distinguish original work from AI-assisted or copied content. Includes a literature review identifying effective models and features, designed use cases, and a collected dataset from student reflections for training and testing.

4190.title: Biodiversity monitoring tool
4190.summary: This project develops deep learning models (including Species Net) for automatic wildlife detection, localization, and segmentation in camera trap images. Addressing challenges like poor image quality and multiple animals, it uses Serengeti and Caltech datasets to create a scalable labeling tool for biodiversity monitoring. Expected outcome: an automated system to assist researchers in large-scale animal identification.

4191.title: Preparing Planslurpen for integration into municipal processes
4191.summary: This master's project focuses on integrating Planslurpen, a machine learning tool that extracts information from zoning plans, into Norwegian municipal workflows. Students will collaborate with Trondheim and Kristiansand municipalities, DiBK, and KS to assess needs and improve the system. Key technologies involve AI/ML, API design, and data infrastructure development. Expected outcomes include proposals or implementations such as a multi-agent AI chatbot, API optimizations, a scalable integration architecture, or methods to enhance retrieval accuracy and user trust through explainability.

4193.title: Measuring the Coding Experience
4193.summary: This project investigates the programming learning process using biometric sensors (EEG, eye tracking, GSR). It involves designing and executing user studies, such as comparing coding tasks with and without AI assistance. The student will analyze multimodal sensor data, potentially creating processing scripts and novel visualizations to interpret cognitive and emotional states. The outcome is an empirical study deriving insights into the coding experience from physiological data. Recommended for two students.

4194.title: Counterfactual Explainability for Human Activity Recognition
4194.summary: This project develops counterfactual explanation methods for time series data, specifically for human activity recognition (HAR) using the wearable sensor HARTH dataset. It addresses challenges of temporal coherence and plausibility. The approach will integrate Transformer and LSTM models from the HARTH-ML repository to generate realistic, actionable counterfactuals. Expected outcomes include a novel counterfactual generation technique, a literature review, implementation, benchmarking against state-of-the-art methods, and exploration of explanation representations. The goal is to improve model interpretability and decision-making in HAR.

4195.title: [NorwAI/NAIL] Symbolic AI for enhancing reasoning and trustworthiness of GPTs
4195.summary: This project investigates neuro-symbolic AI integration, using case-based reasoning to enhance GPT's reasoning, reduce hallucinations, and improve trustworthiness. The student will implement a prototype combining GPT with symbolic systems via fine-tuning or RAG, validated with benchmarks. Key technologies include symbolic AI, LLMs, prompt engineering, and Python data libraries, aiming for a more interpretable and accurate AI system.

4196.title: Parallel Approximate Similarity Join in Vector Databases
4196.summary: Research area: Scalable parallel/distributed approximate similarity join (ASJ) for high-dimensional vectors in vector databases. Technologies: Algorithmic/indexing solutions, implementation in C++, evaluation on large datasets using GPU or distributed systems like Spark. Expected outcomes: Survey existing ASJ methods, develop or extend solutions, implement and empirically evaluate performance. Focus is on algorithmic efficiency, not embedding creation.

4197.title: Visualizing Sepsis Risk Using Clinical Phenotypes
4197.summary: This project develops a visual analytics tool to help clinicians assess sepsis risk by comparing a patient's clinical phenotypes (signs, symptoms, conditions) against historical cohorts. Using electronic health records from St. Olavs Hospital, it involves data analysis and interface design to create an interactive prototype. The expected outcome is a system that aids in early sepsis identification by illustrating patient similarity to known phenotype groups, contributing to personalized risk stratification.

4198.title: Identifying Sepsis Clinical Phenotypes Using Structured and Textual Data
4198.summary: This project aims to identify distinct sepsis patient phenotypes by analyzing both structured electronic health record data and unstructured clinical notes from St. Olavs Hospital. Using techniques like natural language processing and machine learning clustering, the research will group patients based on symptoms, conditions, and hospital events. The expected outcome is a set of clinically meaningful sepsis subgroups to improve treatment personalization and risk stratification.

4199.title: The value of AI
4199.summary: This information systems project investigates how to evaluate the value of AI for society, organizations, and individuals. You will conduct a systematic literature review followed by an empirical qualitative field study (topic to be decided together). The research integrates insights from information systems, computer-supported cooperative work, and human-computer interaction. The expected outcome is a framework for assessing AI value and new empirical knowledge on who benefits and how. The thesis requires strong interest in qualitative research.

4200.title: Generative AI and artistic creation
4200.summary: This project investigates generative AI's dual impact on the arts: its disruptive threat to artistic integrity and traditional markets versus its potential to enhance creativity, audience interaction, and artwork mutability. Research involves a literature review followed by an empirical study using qualitative or quantitative methods to explore these dynamics. A key outcome is the potential development of custom AI tools (e.g., GANs) to assist artistic creation. The thesis will analyze data to understand AI's complex role in transforming artistic processes and engagement.

4201.title: Multimodal Plant Health Monitoring
4201.summary: This project develops a multimodal plant health monitoring system for precision agriculture by integrating temporal IoT soil sensor data (moisture, nutrients, pH) with UAV imagery (RGB, multispectral). Using deep learning and Python, it addresses limitations of single-source systems by fusing disparate spatial-temporal data to improve detection, classification, and prediction of plant health. Expected outcomes include more accurate assessments, early intervention strategies, and optimized resource use for sustainable farming. Collaboration with Nibio may be required for data collection.

4202.title: Digital støtte til flykninger og immigranter i møte med norsk offentlighet
4202.summary: This project focuses on developing digital support tools for refugees and immigrants navigating Norwegian public services. It involves user research and participatory design (co-design) to address diverse needs in accessing bureaucratic information. Expected outcomes include simplified information portals, resource collections, or gamified solutions for youth. Collaboration with public or voluntary service providers is possible. The project emphasizes human-centered, empowering technology design for vulnerable groups. Basic Norwegian understanding is advantageous.

4203.title: DELTA – digitalisering av unges brukerhistorier
4203.summary: This project addresses youth exclusion and lack of understanding of welfare services. It involves co-designing digital tools and methods with young people (16-25) to empower their voice in shaping health and welfare services. Key technologies are user-centered design and qualitative research. Expected outcomes include improved support for youth navigating public aid systems, enhanced service design based on user stories, and ensured data privacy. The project requires skills in empirical qualitative research and design work.

4204.title: MIC - Medical Image Computing / Analysis and AI/CV (most organs / most modalities) (2025-New)
4204.summary: Medical Image Computing projects applying Deep Learning and Computer Vision to analyze multiple organs (e.g., heart, brain) and modalities (CT, MRI, Ultrasound). Development uses the MONAI framework, including models like U-mamba. Collaborative setting with hospitals and research institutes, with opportunities to publish thesis results. Extensive PhD support; work individually or in pairs.

4205.title: Autonomous Driving (AD): NAP-lab related projects based on Visual Intelligence (AI/CV)++ (2025-New)
4205.summary: This project focuses on advancing autonomous driving through AI/computer vision, targeting end-to-end learning (imitation/reinforcement), sim-to-real gap reduction via neural rendering (NeRF/Gaussian splatting), teleoperation backup systems (TeleDrive), HD-map/Digital Twin development for Nordic winters, crowdsourced map updates, and automated camera data anonymization. Research uses a sensor-equipped KIA e-Niro platform, CARLA/NVIDIA DRIVE Sim, and Omniverse. Expected outcomes include functional AD systems in simulation and real-world testing, improved simulation realism, updatable digital twins, and privacy-preserving training data. Opportunities for paper publication and PhD continuation exist.

4206.title: Health of the Herd:  Data-driven decision support for design and planning of environments motivating childhood health and activity
4206.summary: Master's project developing data-driven decision support tools for childhood obesity prevention using anonymized Norwegian demographic, school, and health data at geographical unit levels. Focuses on creating data visualization prototypes to help municipalities, healthcare, and schools design healthier, activity-promoting environments. Collaboration with St. Olav Hospital and Trondheim municipality ensures stakeholder relevance. Expected outcome: functional applications for monitoring and planning interventions to improve child health through environmental design.

4207.title: Deep Learning for Real-time Cardiac Output Monitoring from Arterial Blood Pressure Waveforms
4207.summary: This master's project develops a deep learning system for real-time, continuous cardiac output monitoring from arterial pressure waveforms. The research focuses on creating a hybrid Convolutional-Recurrent Neural Network (CRNN) or exploring advanced architectures like transformers to accurately estimate beat-to-beat stroke volume, particularly during hemodynamic instability. Using public clinical databases (VitalDB, MIMIC-IV), the expected outcome is a validated algorithm that outperforms commercial systems, enabling broader patient access and potentially reducing mortality through timely intervention. The work combines AI innovation with critical care medicine, with the possibility of patentable inventions.

4208.title: ECG-Based Potassium Level Monitoring for ICU Patients: Overcoming the Cold Start Problem
4208.summary: This project develops a deep learning model for continuous, non-invasive potassium level monitoring in ICU patients using ECG data. The core research challenge is overcoming the "cold start" problem—achieving accurate predictions for new patients without prior abnormal data. It uses the MIMIC-IV-ECG dataset and aims to create a robust model, a calibration strategy using only normal initial samples, and a confidence metric system for clinical use, significantly improving upon prior ~60% accuracy.

4209.title: Exploration-oriented Path Query Processing in Property Graphs
4209.summary: This project addresses inefficient result delivery in property graph databases for exploration scenarios. Standard regular path queries return excessively large, similar result sets that provide poor user insight when limited. The research focuses on designing anytime algorithms for variable-length path queries that prioritize result diversity. These algorithms will incrementally compute paths so that even an early, limited result set reveals a useful subgraph structure, while converging to exact complete results given sufficient time. The work involves graph algorithm design for label-constrained path enumeration in large-scale property graphs.

4211.title: Git monitorering i en pedagogisk kontekst med generativ KI
4211.summary: This project explores integrating generative AI into Git-based programming education to automate feedback and assessment. Using GitHub Actions, it aims to develop dashboards for teachers to monitor project data, enhancing pedagogical workflows with well-integrated AI tools.

4212.title: Oppsummering av medstudentvurderinger
4212.summary: This project focuses on developing summarization techniques for peer student assessments to address issues of variable quality, conflicting feedback, and high volume. Building on prior work, it involves implementing and testing a system in a real educational course. Technologies may include text summarization algorithms and educational software. Expected outcomes are improved feedback management through effective presentation of comments and mechanisms for feedback on feedback.

4213.title: *NEW*:  Monitoring wild salmon spawning run by sonar and video (coop. with NINA)
4213.summary: This project automates monitoring of wild Atlantic salmon in the Tana River using AI on sonar and video data. The research involves improving a Python-based sonar tracker (Fishtracker) and developing AI models for video-based fish detection, species recognition, and tracking. Using large historical datasets and new field data, the goal is to create a tool for real-time, automated fish counts, species identification, and size classification to support adaptive fishery management.

4216.title: AI-lab pitched projects - Master-project Safari (2025-New)
4216.summary: This proposal lists diverse AI-focused master's projects across several domains. Research areas include digital health twins (MIA), sports analytics (RBK), medical image computing for pathology and cardiology (MIC), satellite-based environmental monitoring (NINA), and floorplan generation (Schibsted). Core technologies involve AI, machine learning, satellite earth observation, and medical/image data analysis. Expected outcomes are practical AI tools and systems for improved decision-making in healthcare, sports, environmental conservation, and spatial data applications. Projects are flexible for individual or pair completion.

4217.title: Non-destructive Reconstruction of 3D Objects Using Machine Learning
4217.summary: Research area: Enhancing 3D and 4D reconstruction from computed tomography (CT) scans using machine learning. Key technology: Implicit neural representations, building on prior award-winning work (NeCT) for sparse-view and dynamic CT. Expected outcomes: Improved reconstruction quality and temporal resolution (seconds vs. hours) for non-destructive analysis of internal structures, applicable in material science and medical imaging. Project is interdisciplinary, suited for students with strengths in physics/engineering and AI/machine learning.

4218.title: Machine Learning Techniques for Rare Species Detection and Classification
4218.summary: This project applies machine learning to improve rare species detection in bioacoustic data, using the large Sound of Norway bird song dataset. It addresses the poor performance of standard models like BirdNET on underrepresented species. The student will refine models using advanced techniques such as fine-tuning and few-shot learning. The expected outcome is a model with higher accuracy and recall for rare species, enhancing automated biodiversity assessment. Collaboration is with NINA's Miljødata department.

4219.title: System for efficient storage and retrieval of property graphs with time series
4219.summary: Research area: Efficient storage and retrieval of dynamic property graphs with node-attached time series data, relevant to networks like power grids or financial systems. Technologies: Graph databases (e.g., based on existing DBs), algorithm design, performance benchmarking. Expected outcomes: A implemented system for this graph-time series case, evaluated on large datasets to assess storage and query efficiency. Prerequisites: TDT4150/TDT4225, strong programming skills.

4220.title: Hybrid Models Combining Machine Learning and Physical Knowledge - Maximum exploitation of the electricity grid requires robust models of physical units
4220.summary: This project focuses on developing hybrid models that integrate machine learning with physical knowledge to create robust models of electricity grid physical units. It is suited for students in Computer Science, Mathematical Sciences, or Engineering Cybernetics. Supervision will involve a university supervisor with practical guidance from SINTEF, in collaboration with Statnett. The expected outcome is improved modeling to maximize electricity grid exploitation.

4222.title: Explanations in AI decision support systems
4222.summary: This project investigates the use of non-generative black-box AI in Norwegian central government decision support systems. It will analyze post-hoc explainability methods (e.g., LIME, SHAP) to determine what constitutes a useful and legally valid explanation under the Public Administration Act, while quantifying the uncertainty inherent in these approaches. The expected outcome is a framework or guidelines for implementing compliant and reliable AI decision support, developed in collaboration with the Norwegian National Audit Office.

4224.title: *NEW*:  Computer vision for digital twins of ship hulls (cooperation with DNV)
4224.summary: This project develops computer vision methods to create semantic 3D digital twins of ship hulls/tanks from robotic visual data. Research focuses on two areas: 1) segmenting structural components in images/videos using deep learning models and topology rules, and 2) reconstructing a component-separable 3D model from monocular video, validated against Lidar data. Key challenges include low-light conditions. Expected outcomes are a segmentation pipeline and a validated 3D reconstruction method, with a possible summer internship at DNV.

4226.title: Shape Servoing in 3D with Deep Reinforcement Learning – Extending Deformation from 2D to 3D
4226.summary: This project extends Deep Reinforcement Learning-based shape servoing from 2D to full 3D deformation. The research focuses on training a DRL agent with a continuous control policy to manipulate a deformable object, fixed on one side and grasped, into predefined 3D shapes. Using an eye-to-hand camera configuration, the agent must adapt to material properties. Key outcomes include a learned policy for complex elastoplastic deformation, addressing challenges in reward design and variable stiffness. The work builds on a previous 2D framework and requires Python/PyTorch skills.

4227.title: 3D Reconstruction with Active Touch – "Next Best Touch" for Object Classification (with Sintef Ocean)
4227.summary: This project develops a "next best touch" strategy for efficient object classification using tactile data from a GelSight sensor on a robotic arm. It employs machine learning (Python/PyTorch) to learn which touch points maximize classification accuracy after minimal interactions. The expected outcome is a model that accurately infers object identity and reconstructs geometry with few touches, targeting robotic perception in visually occluded or cluttered environments.

4228.title: The Nuts and Bolts of Human-AI Interaction: Riksrevisjonen
4228.summary: This project investigates human oversight requirements for high-risk AI systems under the EU AI Act, using a case study with Norway's National Audit Office (Riksrevisjonen). It employs qualitative research methods (interviews, observations) to analyze how human agency, workflows, and responsibilities must adapt in public sector auditing. Expected outcomes include best practices for effective human-AI collaboration, a critical analysis of Article 14, and insights into implementing oversight to ensure accountability and ethical governance.

4231.title: Orientation Sensing Applied in Accessibility
4231.summary: This project explores orientation sensing in mobile devices to develop an assistive system providing accurate audio navigation for blind users. Research involves studying existing literature, designing and implementing sensor-based prototypes, and conducting user evaluations. The expected outcome is a functional prototype that improves independent mobility through reliable, context-aware audio instructions. The work combines human-computer interaction, mobile sensor technology, and accessibility design.

4232.title: Prediction of Biodiversity of Marine species
4232.summary: This project develops AI methods to automate labeling of marine species images for biodiversity prediction. It uses computer vision (CNNs, ResNet, VGG, transfer learning) on data from frugal (Planktoscope) and high-end (FlowCam) instruments plus satellite data. The goal is to create scalable, robust datasets to model spatial and temporal biodiversity dynamics affected by climate change and human activity, supporting EU coastal mitigation strategies. Co-supervised by Synplan.ai.

4233.title: Efficient Machine-Learning Compiler for Children
4233.summary: This project focuses on designing and implementing an efficient compiler using MLIR to translate visual block-based programming languages (e.g., Scratch, Blockly) into optimized code for a Parallel Ultra-Low-Power RISC-V architecture. The core technology is MLIR within the LLVM framework, requiring strong C++ skills. The expected outcome is a functional compiler toolchain that enables children to run machine learning applications on resource-constrained hardware, involving collaboration with the University of Bologna.

4235.title: AI competences for sustainable transitions
4235.summary: Researching AI and creativity competencies for sustainable transitions within the AI-SECRETT project. Involves literature review, analysis of existing solutions, and iterative design of a tool to support co-design of learning activities. Expected outcome: a validated solution to enhance skills in AI and creativity for social, economic, and environmental sustainability.

4238.title: IMTEL - Generative 3D: AI approaches for creating meshes and textures for educational Augmented Reality experiences
4238.summary: This project explores generative AI for creating 3D meshes and textures to build educational Augmented Reality content. It involves designing a system architecture to integrate API-based 3D generation tools (e.g., TripoSR, Shap-E) with a Python/Django backend and the Unity3D-based MirageXR frontend. The expected outcome is a functional proof-of-concept system, evaluated through a user study, investigating applications in scenarios like medical simulation or language learning for devices such as Apple Vision Pro and HoloLens 2.

4241.title: Multi-objective optimisation for Floor Plan generation
4241.summary: Research area: Multi-objective optimization for automated floor plan generation in computer-aided architectural design. Technologies: Biological-inspired algorithms (e.g., evolutionary computation), potential use of the open-source react-planner visualization tool, and collaboration with Arealize for architectural constraints and furniture database integration. Expected outcomes: Generate feasible, optimized floor plans for office buildings that balance multiple design objectives, with optional integration of interior furniture placement. Students have flexibility in algorithm choice and project focus.

4242.title: Stor skale eller Adaptiv Ruting
4242.summary: This project addresses scalable and adaptive solutions to the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP). It focuses on developing algorithms to solve large-scale instances (thousands of customers with multiple constraints) within strict time limits, and on dynamic routing where real-time solution updates are required due to changing conditions. Students select their specific techniques and research direction. Expected outcomes are efficient, practical routing methods for complex, time-sensitive logistics applications.

4243.title: Deep Evolvable-Substrate HyperNEAT
4243.summary: This project focuses on neuroevolution, combining HyperNEAT, Evolvable-Substrate HyperNEAT (ES-HyperNEAT), and Multi-Spatial Substrate (MSS) into a new framework called Deep Evolvable-Substrate HyperNEAT (DES-HyperNEAT). It evolves deep neural networks by developing and combining multiple substrates, extending ES-HyperNEAT's single-substrate construction. The key technology involves evolutionary algorithms generating network weights based on geometric patterns across substrates. Expected outcomes include the DES-HyperNEAT framework itself and opportunities for students to further extend and improve the technique in new directions.

4244.title: evolusjonære sverm robotikk
4244.summary: The master's project focuses on evolutionary swarm robotics, where groups of autonomous robots solve problems collectively through emergent intelligent behavior. Control mechanisms are evolved using nature-inspired evolutionary methods, such as genetic algorithms, Genetic Network Programming (GNP), and NEAT. Example project directions include: (1) developing altruism via kin recognition to optimize group-level solutions, and (2) evolving foraging behaviors by combining GNP and NEAT. Expected outcomes are adaptive controllers that enable swarms to spread, monitor areas, avoid collisions, communicate, and tackle complex tasks efficiently. Students may extend these examples or propose new research within the theme.

4245.title: Dynamic Computer Vision with Event Cameras: EXCHANGE possibility
4245.summary: This project explores dynamic computer vision using event cameras (neuromorphic/dynamic vision sensors) to address challenges in autonomous navigation. The research focuses on applying bioinspired algorithms to asynchronous event data for trajectory prediction and environment analysis. Collaborating with the University of Waterloo, the work utilizes their event camera datasets and includes an optional exchange semester. Key technologies are event cameras mounted on autonomous vehicles, with expected outcomes in improved visual odometry, SLAM, and tracking for high-motion scenarios without motion blur.

4246.title: Context-aware Foundation Model for Telco Time Series
4246.summary: Developing a context-aware foundation model for telecom multivariate time series to enable multi-task learning (anomaly detection, forecasting, imputation) with minimal fine-tuning. Integrates structured network metrics with unstructured context (tickets, topology, logs) using transformer or diffusion-based architectures and self-supervised objectives like masked modeling. Evaluates cross-domain transferability across network segments and sites using Telenor's real-world data.

4247.title: Time series analysis and forecasting of food loss from grocery stores
4247.summary: This master's project applies time series analysis and forecasting to grocery store food sales and loss data to identify trends (e.g., weekly, monthly, holiday patterns). Technologies include statistical modeling for prediction. Expected outcomes are new strategies to reduce food waste and increase value from unavoidable loss, such as repurposing it as animal feed instead of biogas, contributing to better resource utilization.

4250.title: Biologisk inspirerte metoder for Unsupervised Band Selection for Hyperspectral Images
4250.summary: This project focuses on unsupervised band selection for hyperspectral image processing, addressing high dimensionality and data redundancy. It involves developing biologically inspired optimization techniques, such as Particle Swarm Optimization or Multi-Objective Optimization, with an emphasis on incorporating spatial information. Students will build on prior related work at the department, aiming to improve selection methods and potentially publish results. The project is suited for those interested in evolutionary computation and remote sensing applications.

4251.title: Exploring open boundaries in Artificial Immune systems
4251.summary: This project investigates improving artificial immune systems (AIS) by exploring "open dimensions" – a novel mechanism beyond traditional fixed-shape recognition regions. The research focuses on enhancing data coverage in high-dimensional spaces, a known limitation of current AIS. Technologies involve AIS algorithms and classification. Expected outcomes include a deeper understanding of this mechanism's potential and its application to real-world problems, such as enhancing fake news detection, as demonstrated in related work. Students have flexibility to define their specific direction within this theme.

4252.title: GPS denied visual mapping over open water EXCHANGE possiblity
4252.summary: This project addresses GPS-denied drone navigation over open water by inferring seafloor terrain from observable wave patterns. The research involves using camera-based visual sensing to analyze wave shape and speed, then matching these patterns against bathymetric (seafloor) maps for localization. Bio-inspired or hybrid algorithms may be applied. It is a collaboration with the University of Waterloo, offering an exchange option where the Canadian military provides data. The whole project can also be completed at NTNU with alternative data sources. Expected outcome is a novel visual localization system for open-water environments.

4253.title: Cardio Artery 3D mapping using a single camera EXCHANGE possibility
4253.summary: This project develops a 3D mapping method for cardiology using optical flow from a single moving camera inside arteries, overcoming photogrammetry's parallax limitation. It explores biologically-inspired algorithms to compute depth from time-to-collision. The work is part of a collaboration with the University of Waterloo, offering exchange opportunities for the pre-project or thesis. The expected outcome is a functional system for reconstructing arterial 3D shape from video, with data provision planned.

4254.title: Motion field segmentation for moving cameras in sports EXCHANGE possibilities
4254.summary: Master's project on motion field segmentation for moving cameras in sports, focusing on separating static and moving elements and estimating camera pose. Extends prior work with stationary cameras in ice hockey and baseball to dynamic sports like running and skiing. Uses biological-inspired or hybrid algorithms. Collaboration with University of Waterloo offers data and exchange options. Expected outcomes: enhanced athlete pose, action, and interaction analysis in moving camera scenarios.

4255.title: Designing an Inclusive Mobile App Platform for Improving the Mental Health of Mothers of Children with Intellectual Disabilities
4255.summary: This project designs and tests an inclusive mobile app platform to support mental health for mothers of children with intellectual disabilities. It employs AI-powered, culturally adapted, low-literacy-friendly tools such as visual resources, local-language content, stress management features, and therapist directories. Through needs assessments, prototype development, and usability testing, it aims to produce a feasible solution that addresses barriers of stigma, access, and cost.

4256.title: Local climate information App
4256.summary: This project focuses on developing a mobile app for accessing localized climate information. It involves integrating static geographical data (e.g., Open Street Map) with high-resolution online climate and ecological datasets, such as those from ESA CCI COPERNICUS or E-OBS. The expected outcome is a functional app prototype that visualizes this overlapped data to support decision-making in sectors like agriculture and construction. The work is part of the URSA MAOR project.

4257.title: Using Large Language Models (LLMs) to Improve Healthcare Sustainably - In collaboration with Hugin Medical (and potentially Harvard/MIT researchers)
4257.summary: This project develops an AI system using Large Language Models (LLMs) to reduce unnecessary radiology exams in Norway. You will compare rule-based, supervised machine learning, and LLM approaches to process referral text, connect it to Norwegian clinical guidelines, and generate feedback for doctors. Working with real anonymized hospital data, the goal is a validated tool that flags guideline violations and missing information, improving resource allocation and patient care. Collaboration with Hugin Medical offers industry experience and potential links to Harvard/MIT. Requires Norwegian fluency and basic ML/LLM knowledge.

4259.title: Prosjekter for Trym
4259.summary: Project at SINTEF involving research on Knowledge Acquisition Networks (KANs) and Genetic Programming (GP), supervised by researcher Signe. The research area is likely artificial intelligence or machine learning, focusing on advanced neural network architectures (KANs) and evolutionary algorithms (GP). Specific technologies include these computational methods. Expected outcomes are not detailed but would presumably involve algorithm development, performance analysis, or application case studies within SINTEF's research domains.

4260.title: Explainable Deep Learning for Genomics Discovery
4260.summary: This project combines genome-to-tensor encoding, deep learning architectures, and explainable AI (xAI) to discover higher-order genetic interactions underlying complex diseases. Key challenges involve managing high-dimensional genomic data, representing sparse genetic variants, and selecting appropriate models. The expected outcome is a novel computational framework that identifies previously unknown genetic determinants of disease by interpreting deep neural network decisions, addressing limitations of current genomics analysis methods.

4261.title: LLM-Based Multi-Agent Architecture for Intelligent Embedded Systems
4261.summary: Research Area: IoT embedded systems using LLM-driven multi-agent architectures. Technologies involve Large Language Models powering autonomous agents that control and coordinate IoT devices (sensors, actuators) via natural language and context-aware reasoning. Expected Outcomes: A modular, adaptable embedded system where devices integrate via high-level agent goals, reducing development time and enabling complex edge decision-making through distributed, rule-free coordination.

4262.title: Enhancing Feedback Quality in Software Project Management through LLM adaption
4262.summary: This project explores enhancing automated feedback for software project management education using Large Language Models (LLMs). It investigates prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and few-shot learning to improve feedback relevance and personalization. The research, conducted with EdTech startup InnSpill AI, will produce a taxonomy of prompt patterns, a prototype RAG-enhanced assistant, and empirical insights on LLM effectiveness and user perceptions in educational contexts.

4263.title: Intelligent Systems Engineering of Air Traffic Management Systems
4263.summary: This project explores using Large Language Models (LLMs) to enhance analysis and documentation in Systems Engineering for Air Traffic Management Systems (ATMS). It involves comparative analysis and case studies to assess LLMs for tasks like requirements specification and traceability. Expected outcomes include a thesis addressing research questions on efficiency, pros/cons, and trustworthiness of LLMs, a systematic overview of suitable LLMs for specific ATMS engineering tasks, a documented case study, and a practical user guide for the iTEC collaboration. The work is international, involving Avinor and multiple countries.

4264.title: Reproducibility of News Recommendation Experiments
4264.summary: This project investigates the reproducibility of published results in news recommender systems. The student will re-run experiments using common datasets and algorithms, systematically varying evaluation choices like data splits and metrics. The goal is to assess the reliability of claimed performance improvements in the literature by determining how sensitive results are to methodological decisions.

4265.title: Automated call handling using STT, TTS and AI technologies
4265.summary: This project develops an automated call handling system for inbound traffic using Speech-to-Text, Text-to-Speech, and AI. It focuses on automating inquiries, scheduling, and routing. Key research addresses Norwegian dialect robustness, low-latency multi-turn conversations, and cost/quality trade-offs. Expected outcomes include a functional pipeline, comparative analysis of open-source vs. commercial LLMs for call contexts, and enhanced user experience through personalization and emotion detection.

4266.title: For Eirik og Jørgen: Syntetiske data med SMN1
4266.summary: This project focuses on generating synthetic genetic data for the SMN1 gene, linked to spinal muscular atrophy. It employs machine learning techniques, such as generative models, to create realistic datasets. Expected outcomes include privacy-preserving synthetic data that can augment real genetic data for research and analysis in bioinformatics.

4268.title: Dynamic Neural Billboarding: Simplifying Complex Models for Enhanced GPU Efficiency
4268.summary: This project investigates neural techniques to deform 2D billboards, matching silhouettes of complex 3D objects from any view. The goal is to maximize the largest billboard triangle for improved GPU rendering efficiency. Using simple neural networks or related methods, it may also explore depth/textures for 3D effects and compare alternative approaches. Technologies include neural shape representations like DeepSDF and instant neural graphics. Requires machine learning background and courses in visual computing.

4269.title: Decision Support with Explainable AI (with Sparebank 1 SMN)
4269.summary: Research area: Explainable AI (XAI) for banking decision support. Focuses on interpreting complex models like deep neural networks used for product recommendations. Key technologies: Large-scale dataset (200k records, 2k variables), data analysis, machine learning, and XAI methods (e.g., SHAP, LIME). Expected outcome: Develop and evaluate techniques to provide clear explanations for model predictions, enabling bank employees to understand and trust AI-driven product suggestions. Project in collaboration with Sparebank 1 SMN, with access to provided data and computing resources.

4270.title: Credit Risk Modelling for Credit Card Portfolios
4270.summary: Research area: Credit risk modeling for credit card portfolios, focusing on Probability of Default (PD), Loss Given Default (LGD), and Exposure at Default (EAD). Technologies: Statistical models (logistic regression) and machine learning (Random Forests, XGBoost, Neural Networks) using micro-level customer data and macro-economic indicators. Expected outcomes: Improved PD, LGD, and EAD estimation models, analysis of reward program impacts, and policy recommendations for financial risk management.

4271.title: Approximate Computing & SW Reliability
4271.summary: Master's project on approximate computing and software reliability, combining compiler-level precision tuning with fault-injection and reliability metrics. Aims to develop methodologies for dynamically adjusting computational precision at compile time while using fault-injection to evaluate and maintain software reliability. Expected outcomes include tools or frameworks that optimize efficiency-reliability trade-offs in approximate computing systems, with collaborations from NTNU and Politecnico di Milano.

4272.title: Problemstilling fra Kredittbanken
4272.summary: This project proposes automating the manual review of customer documents (income statements, rental agreements, payment records) for credit applications at Kredittbanken. It will explore technologies like Optical Character Recognition (OCR) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) to extract, validate, and assess information from diverse document formats. The expected outcome is a system that significantly reduces processing time and human error, enabling partial or full automation of the case worker's document assessment workflow.

4274.title: Building Knowledge and Empathy for Accessibility through Educational Games
4274.summary: This project explores using interactive web-based games to teach accessibility concepts and foster empathy. Students will design and implement simulations of impairments (e.g., blindness, motor issues) that demonstrate common web accessibility barriers. The game will guide players to fix these barriers, showing improved usability. Key outcomes include a playable prototype and an evaluation of its learning effectiveness. The work combines web development, human-computer interaction, and inclusive design.

4276.title: ICT for Health & Well-being in Built Environments
4276.summary: This project investigates ICT's role in creating sustainable built environments that enhance occupant health and well-being, within the SWELL project. Research involves literature reviews on ICT for health/well-being and interactive/ubiquitous technologies. The core task is designing and prototyping a digital solution to engage users in physical spaces like buildings, followed by its evaluation. The expected outcome is a working prototype demonstrating how technology can actively promote health and well-being in built settings.

4277.title: Agentic AI for software security
4277.summary: This project explores using multi-agent LLM systems (Agentic AI) to enhance software security analysis, addressing limitations of single-LLM approaches. It involves conducting a systematic literature review and developing prototypes to advance existing Agentic AI methods. The expected outcome is an improved integrated framework or tool for more comprehensive vulnerability detection and remediation in code.

4278.title: Machine Learning-Based Pace Optimization for Formula Student Endurance Racing
4278.summary: This project develops a machine learning model using sensor data to predict optimal real-time driving speeds for Formula Student endurance races. The research focuses on balancing competitiveness with vehicle reliability to assist engineers and drivers. The expected outcome is a functional pace indicator tool to maximize race performance and finishing probability.

4279.title: AI Assisted Active Knowledge Modelling
4279.summary: Research area: AI-assisted active knowledge modelling. Technologies: JavaScript (React, Redux, Shadcn), AI APIs, web-based platforms, standard ontologies (IMF, POSC Caesar, OSDU). Expected outcomes: An AI Modelling App demonstrating collaborative cyclic design, knowledge reuse, and error reduction through intelligent agent integration, resulting in demonstrators that cut modelling time and cost.

4280.title: CERN Collaborations on HPC, AI and/or Big Data
4280.summary: Master's thesis projects in collaboration with CERN, focusing on High-Performance Computing (GPU computing), Artificial Intelligence, and Big Data. Topics are co-defined with CERN for practical applications, with encouragement for participants from the CERN OpenLab summer program.

4281.title: Immersive technologies for eParticipation in urban planning
4281.summary: This master's thesis investigates immersive technologies (AR and VR) for enhancing eParticipation in urban planning. It focuses on developing and evaluating immersive environments to visualize urban designs and facilitate citizen co-design, with a case study on HaakonVII gate in Trondheim via the Enact15mc project.

4282.title: Implementation and Benchmarking of Graph Transformer Architectures
4282.summary: Project focuses on implementing and benchmarking transformer architectures for graph learning. Involves executing one or more published models, collecting performance metrics. Expected outcome: comparative analysis of graph transformer efficiency and accuracy. Targeted for Ulrik Isdahl.

4284.title: Architecture Validation of Hybrid Web2–Web3 Systems Using Simulink
4284.summary: This project focuses on validating hybrid Web2–Web3–SSI system architectures using Simulink and System Composer. It models integrations of traditional web services, blockchain/smart contracts, and Self-Sovereign Identity (verifiable credentials, DIDs). Key concerns include authentication latency, credential flows, data integrity, and fault tolerance. Simulations test scenarios like load, failures, and latency. Expected outcomes: a validated architecture model, identified bottlenecks/failure points, and design recommendations for robust hybrid systems.

4285.title: InterOpt – Integration of Machine Learning and Optimization for Renewable Energy
4285.summary: This project integrates machine learning with optimization models to improve hydropower scheduling in Norway. The intern will use Python to test ML techniques on datasets, aiming to accelerate computational time while maintaining solution quality. Part of the InterOpt research project, it requires skills in Python, ML, and optimization, with an interest in renewable energy systems. The outcome is enhanced decision-making for sustainable energy planning.

4286.title: "Improving Eye Tracker Calibration for People with Visual Impairments" (Spring 2026)
4286.summary: This project develops a configurable VR eye-tracker calibration routine to improve accuracy for users with visual impairments, such as hemispatial neglect. It involves studying existing solutions, prototyping new calibration methods, and evaluating performance against commercial systems. The expected outcome is a validated prototype demonstrating enhanced precision and effectiveness for the target user group.

4287.title: [NorwAI] Using language models for synthesis of dialects
4287.summary: This master's project focuses on speech synthesis for Norwegian dialects using large language models. It involves collecting a dialect dataset, training a synthesis system with Divvun's tool (UiT), and developing a simple chatbot. The work is supervised by experts from the NorwAI research center and the University of Tromsø. Expected outcomes include a functional dialect synthesis system and an evaluated chatbot application.

4288.title: AINCLUSION - Art Exhibition on Women, Inclusion & AI
4288.summary: This project designs and evaluates an interactive art-science exhibition on gender bias and inclusion in AI. It combines storytelling, visual design, and creative AI tools (image/video generation, coding) to build physical/interactive prototypes like games or AR experiences. Research explores how art-based methods can improve public understanding of algorithmic influence and inclusion. Expected outcomes include a museum exhibition concept, insights on engaging awareness methods, and a tested prototype installation for the final show.

4289.title: Growth Model & Business Model for the NGO AINCLUSION
4289.summary: This project develops a sustainable business and growth model for AINCLUSION, an NGO integrating AI and inclusion. Using social entrepreneurship methods, it analyzes best practices, conducts stakeholder interviews, and creates financial forecasts (2026–2030). Expected outcomes include a complete business model, financial plan, partnership map, and a growth roadmap with immediate action recommendations to ensure financial sustainability and maximize social impact.

4291.title: The data governance work around developing robust short-term wind power forecasting
4291.summary: This project explores data governance for robust short-term wind power forecasting, addressing challenges from noisy and missing sensor data in Equinor's operations. Using empirical methods and industry interviews, it aims to develop governance practices supporting deep learning models under degraded data conditions, improving forecast stability and enabling reliable ML deployment in energy systems.
